hypothesis: For enterprise cybersecurity incident triage, a learning-to-rank model trained on SIEM alerts plus incident-response ticket history will prioritize true incidents more effectively than a binary alert classifier, yielding higher precision in the top-k alerts reviewed by analysts.
domain: Cybersecurity operations center (SOC) alert triage for enterprise network intrusion detection
task_prompt:
You would evaluate the hypothesis with a retrospective, production-shaped study built from the organization’s historical SIEM/EDR alert stream joined to incident-response tickets and analyst action logs. Use 18 months of data so the experiment spans multiple detector updates, staffing patterns, and attacker campaigns. Each alert is represented only with information available at alert arrival: native alert fields, detector identity, host/user context, enrichment signals, short-horizon burst statistics, recent entity history, and queue-state variables such as current queue depth. Exclude any analyst-authored or post-disposition fields because they would leak the target.

Construct labels from ticket outcomes. An alert is positive if it is linked within 72 hours to a confirmed incident or a substantive escalation requiring investigation or containment; alerts dispositioned benign are negative. Because the operational task is triage within a live queue rather than isolated scoring, form ranking groups as hourly analyst queues within business unit. This lets the model learn the exact decision problem analysts face: which alerts should be reviewed first among competing contemporaneous candidates.

Use a strict chronological split: train on months 1-12, validate on months 13-15, and test on months 16-18. In this domain, random shuffling would overstate performance because detector tuning, enrichment coverage, and attacker behavior evolve materially over time. Fit all preprocessors only on the training period, then apply them unchanged to later periods. Any rolling-count or prior-history feature must be computed from events strictly preceding the scored alert, and detector metadata introduced later must not be backfilled across split boundaries.

Train a LambdaMART ranker as the proposed model. It is well matched to heterogeneous alert features and the objective of ordering positives ahead of benign items inside each queue. Compare it against a strong binary baseline: a gradient-boosted classifier trained on the same features, with the same train/validation/test periods and comparable hyperparameter effort, then converted to a ranked list by predicted score. Also report the current operational heuristic baseline of vendor severity followed by FIFO ordering, since superiority over today’s process is practically important.

Select models on the validation set using mean NDCG@20 across queues. This metric is appropriate because the SOC’s fixed analyst review budget is concentrated at the top of each queue, and gains in the first handful of positions are far more valuable than improvements deep in the list. Report Precision@5, Precision@20, MRR, recall@20, and AUCPR as secondary diagnostics. Freeze the entire pipeline before touching the final test period and evaluate once, with bootstrap confidence intervals over queues.

To address confounds, report performance by detector family, asset criticality, business unit, and time-of-day. Run feature ablations removing recency and source-product indicators to check whether lift is merely exploiting workflow regularities. Because reviewed alerts are a selected subset, also quantify analyst-review propensity and document where labels are sparse. The experiment will establish whether ranking produces better future triage ordering on historical SOC data, not whether it causally reduces breach impact.

ground_truth.final_verdict: Empirically contested — the design choice at issue cannot be validated without measurement

required_empirical_test.condition: Measure the historical analyst review budget per hour as the median number of alerts reviewed per queue before handoff/rollover. On the validation set, compute both NDCG@20 and Precision@K for the measured K value. Compare whether the model selected by NDCG@20 would differ from the model selected by Precision@K at that exact K. A 0.05 absolute gap in Precision@K between the top-ranked models determines the threshold.
required_empirical_test.supports_critique_if: If the top-ranked model by NDCG@20 has Precision@K at the measured K less than 0.05 higher, or worse, than a model ranked lower by NDCG@20 but better by Precision@K
required_empirical_test.supports_defense_if: If the model with highest NDCG@20 also has Precision@K at the measured K that is at least 0.05 higher than any other model

ideal_debate_resolution.condition: Measure the historical analyst review budget per hour as the median number of alerts reviewed per queue before handoff/rollover. On the validation set, compute both NDCG@20 and Precision@K for the measured K value. Compare whether the model selected by NDCG@20 would differ from the model selected by Precision@K at that exact K. A 0.05 absolute gap in Precision@K between the top-ranked models determines the threshold.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: If the top-ranked model by NDCG@20 has Precision@K at the measured K less than 0.05 higher, or worse, than a model ranked lower by NDCG@20 but better by Precision@K
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: If the model with highest NDCG@20 also has Precision@K at the measured K that is at least 0.05 higher than any other model

must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: The domain-specific challenge of non-stationary alert patterns and workflow drift makes chronological split necessary to mirror deployment conditions

must_not_claim_details.claim: Excluding alerts lacking analyst review reduces sample size unnecessarily
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: Unreviewed alerts have ambiguous labels (neither confirmed incident nor confirmed benign) and would introduce noise in both training and evaluation

hypothesis: In enterprise cybersecurity email security, a transformer model trained on raw email text plus header and URL features will detect phishing and credential-harvesting emails better than a feature-engineered gradient-boosted baseline, as measured by precision-recall AUC on a future-time holdout set.
domain: Cybersecurity — enterprise email security using inbound message text, headers, URLs, and sender reputation logs
task_prompt:
You would evaluate the hypothesis on one year of inbound enterprise email collected at the mail gateway, using only signals available at receipt time: subject, body, MIME/header metadata, extracted URLs, sender/domain reputation logs, attachment indicators, and downstream analyst or incident dispositions. The supervised task is binary phishing/credential-harvesting detection. Positive labels should require confirmed malicious disposition through analyst review, threat-intel enrichment, or incident closure. Negatives should be messages explicitly resolved as benign. Messages that remain merely suspicious or unresolved should be excluded from supervised training so the comparison is not driven by ambiguous labels.

Because this is an adversarial and drifting domain, the split should be chronological: 8 months train, 2 months validation, 2 months test by receipt timestamp. In addition, repeated campaigns are a major leakage risk, so near-duplicate emails must be clustered using normalized subject/body/URL shingles, with each cluster assigned entirely to one split. This prevents a templated phish from appearing in both train and test under minor edits. Any sender reputation or domain-age feature must be snapshotted as of message receipt, never recomputed using future observations.

The proposed model is a multimodal transformer that encodes raw subject and body text and fuses that representation with structured features from headers, authentication outcomes, URL lexical properties, sender/domain reputation buckets, attachment indicators, routing metadata, and basic formatting cues. The baseline should be a strong gradient-boosted tree model using a fair engineered feature set: character and token n-gram summaries, phishing heuristics, URL statistics, SPF/DKIM/DMARC signals, sender mismatch indicators, reputation values, and attachment metadata. Both models should receive comparable tuning effort on the validation set so the comparison is about representation power rather than search asymmetry.

All preprocessing must be fit on the training data only. That includes tokenizer/statistics construction, categorical vocabularies, scaling parameters, and any URL normalization maps. Validation is used for hyperparameter selection and early stopping. The test set remains sealed until all choices are frozen.

The primary metric is precision-recall AUC on the future-time holdout. In this environment the class distribution is highly skewed, and the operational question is whether the model ranks truly malicious emails above benign traffic consistently enough to support stricter analyst queues and user-facing controls. PR-AUC captures that ranking quality in the part of the problem that matters: maintaining useful precision while pushing recall upward. Secondary metrics should include recall at 95% and 99% precision, precision at a fixed analyst-review budget K, ROC-AUC, and calibration measures.

To address confounds, run ablations that remove length and formatting features to verify that gains are not purely template-driven. Report subgroup performance on previously unseen sender domains, unseen URL domains, and reputation strata to test generalization to novel campaigns. This experiment can establish whether the transformer provides better future-time phishing discrimination than a strong engineered baseline under realistic deployment constraints; it does not by itself establish robustness to fully novel attack modalities outside the observed enterprise traffic mix.

ground_truth.final_verdict: Empirically contested — the design choice at issue cannot be validated without measurement

required_empirical_test.condition: Compute precision of both models at the operational threshold (e.g., 99% precision requirement) on the future-time test set; also compute recall at this threshold. Compare precision difference (transformer vs. baseline). If transformer's precision is ≤2 percentage points below baseline at this exact threshold and/or recall is comparable, PR-AUC remains valid. If precision deficit exceeds 2%, PR-AUC is insufficient.
required_empirical_test.supports_critique_if: Transformer model's precision at the operational threshold is more than 2 percentage points lower than the baseline's precision at the same threshold
required_empirical_test.supports_defense_if: Transformer model's precision deficit at the threshold is ≤2% and/or recall is higher
required_empirical_test.ambiguous_if: Transformer model's precision deficit is exactly 2 percentage points below baseline

ideal_debate_resolution.condition: Compute precision of both models at the operational threshold (e.g., 99% precision requirement) on the future-time test set; also compute recall at this threshold. must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: The design correctly restricts labels to confirmed outcomes. In enterprise SOC operations, unresolved messages often lack adjudication due to analyst bandwidth or policy prioritization, making them unreliable indicators of benignity. Including them would create uncontrolled label noise through confirmation bias.

must_not_claim_details.claim: Chronological 8-2-2 split risks underrepresenting rare phishing campaigns in test data
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: This domain-specific choice directly addresses adversarial drift and campaign repetition. The split balances temporal fidelity with cluster-based deduplication to ensure both models evaluate stability across evolving phishing templates rather than memorizing recurring attack patterns.

hypothesis: A graph neural network trained on account-network transaction relationships and merchant/entity links will detect retail banking anti-money-laundering cases with higher precision at the same recall than a tabular gradient-boosted baseline using only per-account transaction aggregates.
domain: Retail banking anti-money-laundering monitoring using transaction streams, merchant metadata, and account-network relationships
task_prompt:
You would evaluate the hypothesis at the account-month level, which matches how an AML monitoring system is typically refreshed and queued for investigators. Build examples from 36 months of historical data drawn from the transaction ledger, customer and account master data, merchant metadata, relationship tables, and case-management outcomes. To prevent label leakage from delayed investigations, define positives only when an account is linked to a SAR or confirmed AML case within 90 days after the prediction timestamp, and exclude examples whose outcomes are not yet fully matured.

Because both transactions and investigations are time-dependent, split the data chronologically: 24 months for training, 6 months for validation, and 6 months for test, with a 90-day maturation buffer before labels are frozen. Every feature must be computed from information available strictly up to the scoring date. Preprocessing statistics, imputers, encoders, and any normalization parameters are fit on the training period only and then applied unchanged to validation and test.

For the tabular baseline, construct a strong feature set from rolling windows over prior activity: transaction count and value, velocity changes, inbound/outbound asymmetry, cash usage, cross-border share, merchant-category composition, counterparty concentration, unusual timing, prior alert history, and customer/profile risk indicators. For the graph model, use the same node features but place them in a heterogeneous graph containing account, merchant, customer/entity, and beneficiary/counterparty nodes. Add edges for transactions and known relationship links, with edge attributes summarizing recency, frequency, and amount over the same historical windows. This ensures the comparison isolates the value of relational message passing rather than differences in raw information.

Train a heterogeneous GNN with two message-passing layers, residual connections, and class-weighted loss to handle the extreme imbalance. Compare it against a gradient-boosted tree baseline tuned with equivalent care on the same validation block. The baseline is intentionally strong because a weaker per-account model would not test whether the gain truly comes from network structure.

Use area under the precision-recall curve as the primary metric. In this setting the bank reviews a ranked queue of accounts, and the practical objective is to push more true suspicious cases toward the top of that queue without inflating the long tail of false alerts. AUPRC captures that ranking quality under severe imbalance better than accuracy or AUROC. Also report precision at several recall levels, recall at investigator-capacity cutoffs, and calibration metrics to make deployment implications visible.

Select hyperparameters and stop training using validation AUPRC only, then run the locked comparison once on the untouched test period. To address confounds, include ablations that remove relationship edges or collapse graph information into simple summary counts, and report subgroup performance across products, geographies, and account tenure. This design establishes whether explicit relational modeling adds incremental detection value over a feature-matched tabular system under realistic temporal evaluation. It does not establish causal drivers of suspicious behavior, and it remains limited by imperfect labels and unlabeled suspicious accounts that were never investigated.

ground_truth.final_verdict: Empirically contested — the design choice at issue cannot be validated without measurement

required_empirical_test.condition: Compute absolute precision difference between the AUPRC-optimized GNN and the best-performing model at two operational thresholds on the test set: (a) investigator capacity cutoff (top K accounts per day, K=average daily reviewed accounts in prior 12 months) and (b) regulatory recall target (Precision@Recall=0.7). The test compares these precision values for both thresholds and checks if either difference exceeds -0.03 (i.e., the AUPRC model's precision is more than 3 percentage points worse than the operationally optimal model at each threshold).
required_empirical_test.supports_critique_if: The AUPRC-optimized model's precision is worse by more than 3 percentage points at either operational threshold compared to the best-performing model for that threshold
required_empirical_test.supports_defense_if: The AUPRC-optimized model's precision is no more than 3 percentage points worse than the operationally optimal model at both thresholds

ideal_debate_resolution.condition: Compute absolute precision difference between the AUPRC-optimized GNN and the best-performing model at two operational thresholds on the test set: (a) investigator capacity cutoff (top K accounts per day, K=average daily reviewed accounts in prior 12 months) and (b) regulatory recall target (Precision@Recall=0.7). The test compares these precision values for both thresholds and checks if either difference exceeds -0.03 (i.e., the AUPRC model's precision is more than 3 percentage points worse than the operationally optimal model at each threshold).
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: The AUPRC-optimized model's precision is worse by more than 3 percentage points at either operational threshold compared to the best-performing model for that threshold
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: The AUPRC-optimized model's precision is no more than 3 percentage points worse than the operationally optimal model at both thresholds

must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: Heterogeneous graphs with multiple node types directly model real-world AML relationships (accounts, merchants, beneficiaries), which align with the hypothesis that relational signal drives detection lift. The design explicitly tests this structural value rather than superficial feature differences.

must_not_claim_details.claim: Chronological splitting with a 90-day label maturation buffer reduces training data and may bias evaluation
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: AML label maturation delay requires temporal holdouts to prevent leakage from delayed investigator outcomes, per stakeholder operational constraints. This split method aligns with the domain's regulatory and production requirements.

hypothesis: For enterprise cybersecurity intrusion detection, a sequence model trained on short windows of network flow and endpoint telemetry will detect novel compromise activity earlier and with higher AUROC than a static feature-based gradient-boosted baseline trained on the same events.
domain: Cybersecurity — enterprise intrusion detection using network flow logs and endpoint/network telemetry from a large corporate environment
task_prompt:
You would evaluate the hypothesis on 18 months of enterprise SIEM/EDR telemetry covering network flows, endpoint process activity, authentication logs, DNS/proxy summaries, and incident-response case outcomes. The prediction unit should be a host-session telemetry window ending before any analyst escalation or containment action. That anchor matters: the main confound in this setting is that post-detection artifacts can make a model look strong while only learning the security team’s response trail. Positive labels come from confirmed compromises in incident closures and post-incident investigations. Negative windows are drawn from assets with no linked incident and no containment activity during a 30-day follow-up period. Weakly adjudicated cases can be used for training under a lower-confidence rule, but they should be excluded from validation and test to keep model comparison tied to cleaner ground truth.

Construct train, validation, and test partitions with a stratified random split at the asset level, preserving prevalence and major asset classes. Grouping by asset prevents the same host from leaking repeated behavioral fingerprints across partitions. In this experiment, the purpose of the split is to compare whether ordered short-horizon behavior carries additional detection signal beyond strong static summaries of the same evidence, so keeping platform mix and class balance aligned across partitions gives the cleanest head-to-head comparison.

For features, derive both models from the same raw telemetry windows. The sequence model receives ordered event tokens and numerical side channels capturing process ancestry, authentication bursts, destination rarity, protocol use, user-host novelty, and rate changes through the window. The static baseline receives aggregated versions of those same signals: counts, histograms, rarity scores, recency summaries, and host/user context features. Any analyst-generated alerts, case references, containment actions, forensic tooling events, or fields populated only after triage are excluded. Preprocessing is fit on training only, including normalization, categorical vocabularies or hashing setup, missingness handling, and sequence length rules.

Train a compact sequence encoder with class-weighted loss and compare it against a gradient-boosted tree baseline on the static aggregates. The baseline should receive equivalent tuning effort so the comparison isolates temporal ordering rather than optimization budget. Use validation AUROC for model selection and early stopping, then touch the test set once for the final comparison.

The primary metric is AUROC because the SOC consumes scores as a ranked queue under changing staffing and alert-budget conditions; the practical question is whether compromised assets move consistently toward the top of review lists. Secondary metrics should include AUPRC, recall at a fixed daily alert budget, false positives per 1,000 host-sessions, Brier score, and median lead time before first analyst escalation so that any ranking gain is checked against operational burden and earlier detection.

Finally, report subgroup results by business unit, operating system family, and asset criticality, plus a held-out check on newly provisioned assets. This design will establish whether short-window sequence modeling adds real detection value on this enterprise’s telemetry while directly guarding against leakage from analyst response artifacts.

ground_truth.final_verdict: Empirically contested — the design choice at issue cannot be validated without measurement

required_empirical_test.condition: Compute the lag-30-day autocorrelation of the daily positive-label rate and of the daily prevalence of top malicious technique families (mapped from incident cases) over the 18-month corpus. If either autocorrelation exceeds 0.30, rerun the evaluation with a chronological split (first 12 months train, next 3 validation, final 3 test) and measure whether the sequence model’s AUROC advantage over the static baseline decreases by more than 0.03 compared to the stratified split result.
required_empirical_test.supports_critique_if: Rerunning with chronological split causes the AUROC advantage of the sequence model over the baseline to decrease by more than 0.03 when either autocorrelation exceeds 0.30
required_empirical_test.supports_defense_if: Either autocorrelation is <=0.30 and no rerun is required, or rerunning with chronological split causes AUROC advantage decrease <=0.03 when autocorrelation exceeds 0.30
required_empirical_test.ambiguous_if: Either autocorrelation is exactly 0.30 and AUROC advantage change is exactly 0.03, or rerun yields no change in AUROC advantage despite autocorrelation exceeding 0.30

ideal_debate_resolution.condition: Compute the lag-30-day autocorrelation of the daily positive-label rate and of the daily prevalence of top malicious technique families (mapped from incident cases) over the 18-month corpus. If either autocorrelation exceeds 0.30, rerun the evaluation with a chronological split (first 12 months train, next 3 validation, final 3 test) and measure whether the sequence model’s AUROC advantage over the static baseline decreases by more than 0.03 compared to the stratified split result.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: Either autocorrelation is <=0.30 and no rerun is required, or rerunning with chronological split causes AUROC advantage decrease <=0.03 when autocorrelation exceeds 0.30
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: Either autocorrelation is exactly 0.30 and AUROC advantage change is exactly 0.03, or rerun yields no change in AUROC advantage despite autocorrelation exceeding 0.30

must_not_claim_details.claim: Excluding analyst-generated alerts and containment actions from features removes critical intrusion signals that correlate with compromise status.
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: These elements represent post-detection artifacts created by analyst response actions and contain information unavailable at detection time. Including them would leak response trail patterns into model training, creating deceptive performance metrics that measure recognition of investigation activities rather than proactive compromise detection.

must_not_claim_details.claim: Using a compact sequence model with limited architecture capacity restricts the model's ability to learn complex intrusion behaviors.
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: The hypothesis explicitly tests performance under realistic SOC deployment constraints. Using a moderate-capacity model ensures the architecture comparison reflects operational limitations and eliminates capacity-biased advantages for sequence models, maintaining focus on temporal modeling value alone.

sound_design_reference:
You would evaluate the hypothesis on 18 months of enterprise SIEM/EDR telemetry covering network flows, endpoint process activity, authentication logs, DNS/proxy summaries, and incident-response case outcomes. The prediction unit should be a host-session telemetry window ending before any analyst escalation or containment action. That anchor matters: the main confound in this setting is that post-detection artifacts can make a model look strong while only learning the security team’s response trail. Positive labels come from confirmed compromises in incident closures and post-incident investigations. Negative windows are drawn from assets with no linked incident and no containment activity during a 30-day follow-up period. Weakly adjudicated cases can be used for training under a lower-confidence rule, but they should be excluded from validation and test to keep model comparison tied to cleaner ground truth.

Construct train, validation, and test partitions with a stratified random split at the asset level, preserving prevalence and major asset classes. Grouping by asset prevents the same host from leaking repeated behavioral fingerprints across partitions. In this experiment, the purpose of the split is to compare whether ordered short-horizon behavior carries additional detection signal beyond strong static summaries of the same evidence, so keeping platform mix and class balance aligned across partitions gives the cleanest head-to-head comparison.

For features, derive both models from the same raw telemetry windows. The sequence model receives ordered event tokens and numerical side channels capturing process ancestry, authentication bursts, destination rarity, protocol use, user-host novelty, and rate changes through the window. The static baseline receives aggregated versions of those same signals: counts, histograms, rarity scores, recency summaries, and host/user context features. Any analyst-generated alerts, case references, containment actions, forensic tooling events, or fields populated only after triage are excluded. Preprocessing is fit on training only, including normalization, categorical vocabularies or hashing setup, missingness handling, and sequence length rules.

Train a compact sequence encoder with class-weighted loss and compare it against a gradient-boosted tree baseline on the static aggregates. The baseline should receive equivalent tuning effort so the comparison isolates temporal ordering rather than optimization budget. Use validation AUROC for model selection and early stopping, then touch the test set once for the final comparison.

The primary metric is AUROC because the SOC consumes scores as a ranked queue under changing staffing and alert-budget conditions; the practical question is whether compromised assets move consistently toward the top of review lists. Secondary metrics should include AUPRC, recall at a fixed daily alert budget, false positives per 1,000 host-sessions, Brier score, and median lead time before first analyst escalation so that any ranking gain is checked against operational burden and earlier detection.

Finally, report subgroup results by business unit, operating system family, and asset criticality, plus a held-out check on newly provisioned assets. This design will establish whether short-window sequence modeling adds real detection value on this enterprise’s telemetry while directly guarding against leakage from analyst response artifacts.
difficulty_justification: The ambiguous choice involves recognizing that analyst-confirmed intrusion labels in enterprise telemetry naturally contain temporal dependencies at the incident level. A careful methodologist must connect these temporal dependencies to potential overfitting in non-chronological splits, requiring analysis of how post-detection artifacts manifest over time. This is a medium difficulty because the design explicitly emphasizes detection timing and the danger of learning from detection trail artifacts, providing methodological clues for identifying the ambiguity in split method.

hypothesis: For enterprise cybersecurity SOC alert triage, a learning-to-rank model that uses incident-response ticket text plus SIEM/EDR alert metadata and historical case outcomes will prioritize true high-severity incidents more effectively than a binary classifier that predicts alert maliciousness and then sorts by predicted probability, as measured by precision@k and mean time-to-triage on held-out future alerts.
domain: Cybersecurity operations center (SOC) alert triage using SIEM/EDR alert streams and incident response tickets
task_prompt:
You would evaluate the hypothesis on 18 months of historical SOC data joined across SIEM/EDR alerts, enrichment records, and incident-response tickets. The unit of prediction is an individual alert at creation time. Labels come from the final linked case outcome, mapped to a three-level relevance target: high-severity confirmed incident, lower-severity confirmed security incident, or non-incident. Because SOC datasets contain repeated alert copies and multi-alert incidents, exact duplicate alerts within a short window should be collapsed and all alerts tied to the same incident kept in the same split.

The split should be strictly chronological: 12 months train, 3 months validation, 3 months test. This is the right evaluation frame for SOC operations because attacker behavior, detections, and analyst playbooks drift over time; the model should be judged on future queues, not random re-samplings of the past. Leakage is controlled by grouping on incident ID across splits and by constructing historical aggregates only from events preceding each alert timestamp.

Feature construction should combine the information analysts actually have when triaging. From text, use alert titles, rule descriptions, and analyst notes entered before final disposition, represented with TF-IDF unigrams and bigrams. From structured telemetry, include detector family, product source, asset criticality, host role, user risk markers, process ancestry, network destination reputation, MITRE tags, and basic timing fields. Add causal history features such as prior alerts on the same asset or identity, prior incident counts, and recent detector escalation rates. Exclude closure notes, containment actions, and any fields populated after investigation because they encode the outcome.

The proposed model is LambdaMART trained in a pairwise ranking setup, with analyst work queues defined by shift-hour buckets so each training query corresponds to alerts competing for analyst attention at the same time. Hyperparameters are tuned on the validation window. The baseline is a gradient-boosted binary classifier trained on the identical features to predict maliciousness, then sorted by predicted probability. Matching features and tuning budget ensures the comparison tests ranking formulation rather than input richness.

The primary metric is Precision@20 averaged across queues. In this SOC setting, success depends on whether the first page of alerts contains more truly urgent incidents, because analyst attention is scarce and queue head quality matters more than global probability calibration. Report NDCG@20, MRR, Precision@50, and an offline replay estimate of mean time-to-triage as secondary metrics. Model selection is done on validation Precision@20 with NDCG@20 as a tie-breaker, and the test window is touched once after all choices are frozen.

To address confounds, report subgroup performance by detector family, asset criticality, and rule age, and include a no-text ablation. This design will establish whether a ranking objective improves future alert prioritization on observed SOC outcomes under realistic drift. It will not establish a fully unbiased estimate of latent attack risk, since analyst investigation depth influences which cases receive definitive severity labels.

ground_truth.final_verdict: Empirically contested — the design choice at issue cannot be validated without measurement

required_empirical_test.condition: Audit 30 days of production triage logs to measure the distribution of alerts-per-queue processed before analyst refresh. If at least 80% of analyst queues fall within 15–25 alerts worked per cycle, Precision@20 is an adequate primary metric. Otherwise, compare the ranking model's precision at the observed operational alert count and score threshold against the baseline under the same threshold.
required_empirical_test.supports_critique_if: Fewer than 80% of queues fall within 15–25 alerts, and the ranking model's precision at the observed operational cutoff trails the baseline by >3 percentage points.
required_empirical_test.supports_defense_if: At least 80% of queues fall within 15–25 alerts, or the ranking model's precision at the observed cutoff remains within 3 percentage points of the baseline despite differing queue structures.

ideal_debate_resolution.condition: Audit 30 days of production triage logs to measure the distribution of alerts-per-queue processed before analyst refresh. If at least 80% of analyst queues fall within 15–25 alerts worked per cycle, Precision@20 is an adequate primary metric. Otherwise, compare the ranking model's precision at the observed operational alert count and score threshold against the baseline under the same threshold.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: At least 80% of queues fall within 15–25 alerts, or the ranking model's precision at the observed cutoff remains within 3 percentage points of the baseline despite differing queue structures.

must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: Chronological splitting mirrors real-world concept drift in SOC operations. Random splits would incorrectly assume stationarity in attacker behavior and detection rules, creating an unrealistic evaluation framework for this domain.

must_not_claim_details.claim: Excluding post-disposition analyst notes creates data leakage
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: Post-disposition notes reflect investigation outcomes rather than real-time observations available during triage. Including them would encode label information into features, violating the temporal validity of the experiment.

hypothesis: For enterprise cybersecurity intrusion detection, a graph neural network built on the entity-relationship graph of users, hosts, IPs, processes, and authentication events will detect malicious activity more accurately than a tabular gradient-boosted baseline trained on the same raw event features, especially when evaluated on future-time windows that contain previously unseen attack campaigns.
domain: Enterprise cybersecurity intrusion detection using network flow logs and endpoint/network telemetry from a large corporate environment
task_prompt:
Use 18 months of telemetry from one enterprise environment so the comparison reflects the data generating process the SOC actually faces. Construct host-day examples, because this unit is operationally meaningful for triage and allows both relational and non-relational models to score the same decision target. Labels come from incident-response-confirmed malicious activity: mark a host-day positive when the host falls within seven days of a confirmed malicious interval, using ticket timelines and forensic scoping to align telemetry with the incident. Because security labels are delayed and imperfect, exclude host-days near unresolved investigations and remove analyst-authored fields, ticket identifiers, suppression states, and any post hoc annotations that could leak outcome information.

Split data strictly by time: train on months 1-11, validate on months 12-15, and test on months 16-18. In addition, keep all windows tied to the same incident or campaign in the same split based on first observed malicious activity, so later stages of one intrusion cannot bleed into development while earlier stages appear in evaluation. This is essential here because the hypothesis is explicitly about future-time generalization to unseen campaigns, not retrospective reconstruction of known attacks.

Build two representations from the same underlying event stream. The tabular baseline uses host-day aggregates: authentication anomalies, process rarity, parent-child execution patterns, lateral movement indicators, network connection novelty, destination reputation buckets, privilege changes, and rolling behavioral deltas, joined with asset criticality and identity metadata. The graph model uses those same raw signals as typed node and edge attributes in a heterogeneous graph over users, hosts, IPs, processes, and authentication sessions formed from the prior seven days of telemetry. All preprocessing, vocabularies, normalization statistics, and frequency cutoffs are fit on training only and then frozen.

Train a heterogeneous GNN to classify each host-day, tuning depth, hidden width, neighbor sampling, class weighting, and learning rate on the validation period. Compare it against a gradient-boosted tree baseline with matched tuning effort over standard tree hyperparameters and class weighting. This is the appropriate baseline because GBDTs are the strongest conventional choice for mixed enterprise telemetry once relations are flattened; if the GNN wins, the gain is credibly attributable to relational modeling rather than simply using richer metadata.

Use AUPRC as the primary metric. In this setting the SOC reviews a ranked alert queue and daily review capacity changes with staffing, incident load, and business criticality. The experiment asks whether the GNN surfaces true intrusions earlier in the queue across future attack windows, and AUPRC captures concentration of positives over the full ranking under extreme class imbalance better than AUROC.

Also report recall at fixed false-positive rate, precision at top-K hosts per day, AUROC, and Brier score so ranking, low-alert-budget performance, and calibration are all visible. Perform model selection on validation AUPRC with early stopping, freeze all choices, and touch the test set once at the end. Finally, run controls for host class, business unit, and campaign novelty, plus an ablation that removes graph edges while preserving node features to test whether any observed gain comes from structure rather than metadata alone. The experiment establishes whether graph-based relational inductive bias improves forward-looking intrusion detection in this environment; it does not by itself prove transfer to other enterprises with different telemetry coverage or investigation practices.

ground_truth.final_verdict: Empirically contested — the design choice at issue cannot be validated without measurement

required_empirical_test.condition: Analyze historical alert volume thresholds across 3 months of production days. For each day, compute the daily alert volume threshold used by analysts to triage hosts. Identify the dominant operational threshold as the median alert volume selected across days where alert volume falls within ±10% of the median. If at least 80% of production days align with this threshold, then: (1) calculate precision, recall, and recall at FPR ≤ 0.001 for both models at this threshold on the test set, (2) determine whether the relative performance difference between GNN and GBM across these metric deltas exceeds 5 percentage points of recall or 10 percentage points of precision compared to their AUPRC-based ordering.
required_empirical_test.supports_critique_if: The GNN shows higher AUPRC but has ≥5% lower recall or ≥10% lower precision at the dominant operational threshold compared to GBM
required_empirical_test.supports_defense_if: The GNN maintains ≤5% recall and ≤10% precision differences at the threshold compared to AUPRC-based ranking

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: The GNN maintains ≤5% recall and ≤10% precision differences at the threshold compared to AUPRC-based ranking

must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: Label leakage through incident ticket IDs and suppression states creates artificial performance gains. The design correctly removes these to prevent models from exploiting outcome-correlated artifacts and ensures evaluation reflects genuine detection capability.

must_not_claim_details.claim: Stratifying split based on first-seen incident time introduces look-ahead bias by reserving follow-up windows

By anchoring incidents to first-seen timestamps and clustering follow-up windows, the split prevents temporal leakage from later stages of an intrusion into development while ensuring evaluation mirrors real deployment conditions.

The experiment establishes whether richer text-plus-structure modeling improves phishing ranking and low-FPR detection on future enterprise mail...

If the model with higher AUROC is worse than the alternative by more than 5 percentage points in precision or 3 percentage points in TPR at FPR <= 0.1%, AUROC is insufficient as the primary selection metric.

hypothesis: For enterprise email security, a transformer model fine-tuned on message text plus header and URL features will detect spear-phishing emails better than a gradient-boosted baseline using only engineered metadata, measured by PR-AUC on a temporally held-out test set.
domain: Cybersecurity — enterprise email security using inbound message text, headers, URLs, and sender reputation logs
task_prompt:
Use 12 months of inbound mail-gateway data from a single enterprise and join each message to the telemetry available at receipt time: raw subject/body text, routing and authentication headers, sender and recipient metadata, URL and attachment indicators, and point-in-time sender/domain/IP reputation logs. Define positives as emails confirmed malicious or spear-phishing by analysts within 14 days of receipt. Define negatives from messages explicitly resolved as benign or bulk/spam and never later escalated. Exclude unlabeled or weakly adjudicated traffic from supervised training so the comparison is driven by reliable outcomes.

Because attacker tactics and local mail policy drift over time, split strictly by receipt timestamp: train on months 1-8, validate on months 9-10, and reserve months 11-12 as a final future-like test. Before splitting, cluster near-duplicate messages and campaign variants using normalized body similarity, shared URL infrastructure, and subject/header overlap, then assign each cluster wholly to one partition based on its first appearance. This prevents the model from seeing one copy of a phishing wave during training and another in test. All transformations are fit on training only, including tokenization decisions, imputers, and categorical encodings.

Train the proposed model by fine-tuning a pretrained transformer on subject and body text, then concatenate the pooled text representation with structured features from headers, URLs, attachments, sender-recipient history, authentication outcomes, and reputation snapshots. Feed the combined representation through a small classification head. Compare against a strong gradient-boosted tree baseline built on engineered metadata only, excluding free text so the experiment directly tests whether message language plus structured context adds detection value beyond conventional gateway features. Give both models comparable tuning effort on the validation window.

Use PR-AUC as the primary metric on the temporally held-out test set. In this setting, analysts consume a ranked queue of suspicious emails rather than a single fixed decision threshold, and the practical objective is to place truly malicious messages as high in that queue as possible despite extreme class imbalance. PR-AUC therefore captures whether the new model improves triage quality across the range of alert volumes the security team can realistically review. To make the result operationally interpretable, also report recall at fixed alert-volume budgets, precision in the top 500 and top 1000 weekly alerts, AUROC, calibration diagnostics, and campaign-level recall after deduplication.

Perform model selection and early stopping only on the validation period, choosing the best checkpoint and hyperparameters by PR-AUC. Touch the test set once at the end. To address confounds, run ablations that remove reputation features and, separately, remove body text so any gain can be attributed more precisely to learned language versus sender cues. Also report slice performance on previously unseen sender domains and previously unseen campaign clusters. This design establishes whether the multimodal transformer gives better future-period phishing ranking than a strong metadata baseline under realistic drift and duplicate-control constraints; it does not by itself prove robustness across organizations with different mail mixes or analyst labeling practices.

ground_truth.final_verdict: Empirically contested — the design choice at issue cannot be validated without measurement

required_empirical_test.condition: Compute the percentage of analyst actions observed in the top 0.5% of alerts from validation scores. Compare transformer and baseline model precision at this fixed budget threshold on the test set: if >80% of analyst actions fall within this budget and the transformer's precision is 2+ percentage points lower than the baseline's, then PR-AUC alone is insufficient as the primary metric.
required_empirical_test.supports_critique_if: Validation data shows >80% of analyst actions occur within the top 0.5% of alerts AND the transformer's precision at this threshold is 2+ percentage points below the baseline
required_empirical_test.supports_defense_if: Validation data shows ≤80% of analyst actions within the top 0.5% of alerts OR the transformer's precision at this threshold is within 2 percentage points of the baseline
required_empirical_test.ambiguous_if: Validation data shows exactly 80% of analyst actions within the top 0.5% budget AND the transformer's precision difference is exactly 2 percentage points

ideal_debate_resolution.condition: Compute the percentage of analyst actions observed in the top 0.5% of alerts from validation scores. Compare transformer and baseline model precision at this fixed budget threshold on the test set: if >80% of analyst actions fall within this budget and the transformer's precision is 2+ percentage points lower than the baseline's, then PR-AUC alone is insufficient as the primary metric.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: Validation data shows >80% of analyst actions occur within the top 0.5% of alerts AND the transformer's precision at this threshold is 2+ percentage points below the baseline
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: Validation data shows ≤80% of analyst actions within the top 0.5% of alerts OR the transformer's precision at this threshold is within 2 percentage points of the baseline
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: Validation data shows exactly 80% of analyst actions within the top 0.5% budget AND the transformer's precision difference is exactly 2 percentage points

must_not_claim_details.claim: Restricting evaluation to analyst-confirmed emails introduces selection bias
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: The hypothesis specifically tests spear-phishing detection performance under realistic labeling constraints. Most false positives never get escalated, so using uncertain labels would create measurement noise rather than reflecting SOC operational reality.

must_not_claim_details.claim: Chronic clustering of campaign emails creates overly conservative performance estimates
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: Duplicate control prevents optimistic leakage but mirrors real-world deployment where models face novel campaigns. The security team must detect unseen attacks, making this clustering protocol appropriate for the hypothesis.

To make the result operationally interpretable, also report recall at fixed alert-volume budgets, precision in the top 500 and top 1000 weekly alerts, AUROC, calibration diagnostics, and campaign-level recall after deduplication.

Perform model selection and early stopping only on the validation period, choosing the best checkpoint and hyperparameters by PR-AUC. Touch the test set once at the end. To address confounds, run ablations that remove reputation features and, separately, remove body text so any gain can be attributed more precisely to learned language versus sender cues. Also report slice performance on previously unseen sender domains and previously unseen campaign clusters. This design establishes whether the multimodal transformer gives better future-period phishing ranking than a strong metadata baseline under realistic drift and duplicate-control constraints; it does not by itself prove robustness across organizations with different mail mixes or analyst labeling practices.

hypothesis: For enterprise cybersecurity SOC alert triage, a learning-to-rank model that incorporates incident response ticket history and alert graph connectivity will prioritize true malicious alerts above a flat binary classifier trained on the same raw alert fields, yielding higher top-k precision on a chronological holdout period.
domain: Cybersecurity operations center (SOC) alert triage for enterprise network intrusion detection
task_prompt:
You would evaluate the hypothesis with a production-faithful retrospective study built from 18 months of SIEM and EDR alert streams joined to analyst dispositions, incident response tickets, and alert-linkage metadata. The unit of ranking is the alert as seen at triage time. Labels should reflect confirmed maliciousness rather than mere analyst attention, so an alert is positive if it is marked malicious by an analyst or attached to an incident ticket that is ultimately confirmed malicious within 14 days. Alerts explicitly closed benign are negatives. Unresolved and weakly reviewed alerts are excluded from supervised fitting to avoid forcing noisy negatives, but they remain in descriptive analyses so you can quantify coverage and selection bias.

Because SOC data drift with attacker behavior, detection content, and analyst practice, the split should be strictly chronological: months 1-12 for training, 13-15 for validation, and 16-18 for test. Alerts tied to the same incident or case must stay in the same split to prevent leakage through duplicated campaigns. Every transform is fit on the training window only. Feature computation must also respect event time: graph and history features are derived only from alerts and tickets available up to each alert timestamp, never from future case closures or later-linked alerts.

The feature set has three parts. First are raw alert fields available immediately at triage, such as rule name, product, severity, host class, user type, alert volume, and diurnal context. Second are incident-history features summarizing prior security activity for the same host, user, subnet, or rule over trailing windows. Third are graph-connectivity features that capture whether an alert sits inside a dense connected structure of related entities or near previously confirmed malicious activity. Fields that encode analyst workflow after triage, including queue assignment, final closure metadata, or responder identity, are excluded.

For the proposed method, train a LambdaMART ranker with hourly analyst triage windows as query groups so the model learns to order alerts within realistic review batches. Use a gradient-boosted binary classifier on the same raw alert fields as the baseline, with comparable hyperparameter search budget and probability-based sorting. This isolates the hypothesis: whether ranking with historical and graph context outperforms a flat alert-only classifier.

Model selection is done on the validation block using NDCG@20, with early stopping and predeclared ablations for raw-only, raw-plus-history, and full features. The final test block is touched once after all decisions are frozen. Primary reporting uses NDCG@20 because analysts act on the head of the queue, and the experiment asks whether malicious alerts are lifted into that reviewed slice. Secondary metrics include Precision@5, Precision@20, MRR, and AUCPR.

To address confounds, compare performance on first-seen entities, on newly introduced rules, and across business units and detection sources. Also report whether gains remain after controlling for simple recency and alert-volume effects. This experiment establishes whether the added context improves prioritization on future SOC traffic; it does not by itself prove reduced analyst workload or incident containment time without an online study.

ground_truth.final_verdict: Empirically contested — the design choice at issue cannot be validated without measurement

The test is resolved if NDCG@20 improvement exceeds 0.03 AND Precision@K improvement is less than 0.01.

required_empirical_test.supports_defense_if: NDCG@20 improves by ≤0.03 OR Precision@K improves by ≥0.01

hypothesis: For enterprise cybersecurity email defense, a transformer model fine-tuned on raw email text plus header metadata and sender-reputation features will detect phishing emails better than a baseline gradient-boosted model trained only on engineered URL, domain, and attachment features, as measured by PR-AUC on a future-time holdout set.
domain: Cybersecurity — enterprise email security using inbound message text, headers, URLs, and sender reputation logs
task_prompt:
You would evaluate the hypothesis on 18 months of inbound mail-gateway data from one enterprise, using only signals available at message receipt time. The dataset includes subject and body text, parsed header fields, embedded URL metadata, attachment metadata, and sender-reputation logs. Because labels in this environment mature slowly, you should include only messages whose case outcome is finalized and impose a 21-day maturation window before extraction so messages later escalated to phishing are not treated as benign. The binary target is phishing versus non-phishing, where phishing includes credential theft, malware delivery, business email compromise, and malicious lures confirmed by analysts.

The split should be strictly chronological: 12 months train, 3 months validation, 3 months test by receipt timestamp. In email defense, campaign signatures, sender infrastructure, and lure language drift continuously, so the correct question is whether a model trained on past mail ranks future mail better. To prevent inflated estimates from campaign duplication, remove exact duplicates before splitting and group near-duplicates using normalized URL sets and subject hashes so variant copies of the same campaign do not appear in both training and evaluation windows.

For the proposed model, fine-tune a pretrained transformer on subject plus body text, and concatenate pooled text embeddings with structured header and sender-reputation features before the final classifier. Useful structured inputs include SPF/DKIM/DMARC outcomes, Reply-To mismatch indicators, sending IP geography/ASN, MIME irregularities, historical sender complaint rates, first-seen age, prior malicious verdict rate, and burstiness features computed only from events preceding each message. All tokenizers, imputers, frequency encoders, and scalers are fit on training data only and then frozen.

The baseline should be a strong gradient-boosted tree model over the structured artifact features that production systems commonly rely on: URL lexical characteristics, domain/TLD and reputation features, redirect and obfuscation indicators available at receipt, attachment type and macro/archive signals, plus lightweight header anomaly counts. Give the baseline equivalent tuning effort on the same validation window so the comparison tests the added value of raw-text representation learning rather than differential optimization effort.

Use PR-AUC as the primary metric on the final future-time holdout because phishing is rare and the security queue operates on a ranked stream of candidates. Secondary reporting should include precision at 90% recall, recall at a fixed false-positive rate per 10,000 messages, ROC-AUC, and calibration metrics such as Brier score and expected calibration error. Select hyperparameters and early stopping solely on validation PR-AUC, freeze the pipeline, and touch the test set once at the end.

Finally, control for confounds that could mimic semantic improvement. Report a seen-versus-unseen sender-domain breakdown, plus strata for link-only versus attachment-bearing messages and recipient business unit. This experiment establishes whether the transformer stack improves future phishing detection under realistic enterprise conditions; it does not, by itself, prove that the gain comes exclusively from deeper language understanding rather than a combination of text and richer contextual cues.

ground_truth.final_verdict: Empirically contested — the design choice at issue cannot be validated without measurement

hypothesis: For vulnerability management in enterprise endpoint/server fleets, a learning-to-rank model that combines asset inventory, network topology, scanner findings, and exploitability signals will surface truly urgent remediation items more effectively than ranking by raw CVSS score alone, as measured by prioritized discovery of vulnerabilities that are actually exploited or addressed within the next remediation cycle.
domain: Cybersecurity vulnerability management for enterprise endpoint and server fleets using asset inventory, network topology, and vulnerability scanner data
task_prompt:
The experiment should be framed around the actual remediation decision unit: a weekly queue of open vulnerability findings competing for limited analyst attention. Build a 12-month panel by joining scanner outputs, CMDB records, network exposure/topology metadata, exploit intelligence, and remediation tickets at the vulnerability-instance level. A record is created for each asset-CVE finding that is open at the start of a weekly remediation cycle. Labels are assigned from events occurring strictly after that cutoff: a finding is positive if it is confirmed exploited or remediated within the next 30 days, and negative otherwise. This definition reflects practical urgency while keeping feature construction temporally clean.

Use a chronological split by week, with the earliest 70% of cycles for training, the next 15% for validation, and the final 15% for test. This is necessary because scanner observations, exploit feeds, and remediation behavior evolve over time. Keep all findings from the same asset-week in the same split, and derive every feature from information available at cycle start only. Fit imputers, encoders, and normalizers on training data only, then freeze them for validation and test.

Feature engineering should combine four signal families. First, vulnerability-level signals: CVSS components, scanner severity, finding age, persistence across prior scans, software family, and whether the CVE is present in exploit intelligence. Second, asset context: business criticality, owner team, server versus endpoint role, OS generation, patch cadence, and prior remediation responsiveness. Third, exposure/topology: internet exposure, subnet sensitivity, reachability to crown-jewel segments, local degree/centrality summaries, and counts of neighboring exposed assets. Fourth, queue context: number of open findings on the asset, concentration of similar CVEs, and backlog burden for the owning team. Exclude closure fields, future persistence indicators, and any post-cutoff ticket updates.

Train a LambdaMART ranker with query groups defined by remediation cycle and business unit so the model learns the ordering problem security teams actually face. Compare it against two baselines: raw CVSS score ranking, which is the incumbent policy being challenged, and a tuned gradient-boosted pointwise classifier over the same features, ranked by predicted probability. Give the classifier comparable tuning effort so any advantage is attributable to ranking formulation rather than optimization asymmetry.

Use NDCG@100 as the primary metric because operations teams act on a bounded top segment of the queue, and the metric rewards placing the most consequential items early while allowing higher gain for exploit-confirmed positives than for routine remediations. Track Recall@100 for exploit-confirmed findings, MAP@100, MRR, PR-AUC, and top-decile lift as secondary diagnostics. Select models on validation NDCG@100 with early stopping, and touch the test set once at the end.

To address confounds, run ablations that remove CVSS-derived fields, recency features, and asset criticality/exposure variables, then check whether the learned ranker still outperforms CVSS alone. Also report performance by asset class, internet exposure, and business unit to verify that gains are not concentrated only in obviously high-priority segments. The study establishes whether integrated contextual ranking improves prioritization under realistic queue constraints; it does not by itself prove causal reduction in incidents after deployment.

ground_truth.final_verdict: Empirically contested — the design choice at issue cannot be validated without measurement

If the proposed model's NDCG@100 improvement over the baseline exceeds 0.03 while its Precision@K improvement is ≤ 0.01 or negative, NDCG@100 is insufficient as the sole primary metric.

hypothesis: For enterprise cybersecurity email defense, a transformer model fine-tuned on raw inbound message text plus headers and URL features will detect malicious/phishing emails with higher PR-AUC and lower false-negative rate than a baseline gradient-boosted model built only on handcrafted lexical and sender-reputation features.
domain: Enterprise cybersecurity email defense in a large organization’s inbound mail gateway
task_prompt:
Use a retrospective but deployment-faithful evaluation on 18 months of inbound mail-gateway traffic from the enterprise environment where the model would be used. Construct the dataset from externally originated inbound messages and retain safe text extracts, subject lines, key headers, URL/domain indicators, attachment metadata, and downstream SOC verdicts. Because labels arrive late, freeze labels only after a 30-day maturation period; messages still under investigation at freeze time should be excluded rather than forced into the negative class. Positives are analyst-confirmed phishing or malicious emails. Negatives are messages with no malicious disposition or incident linkage after maturation.

Split the data chronologically by receipt time: 12 months train, 3 months validation, 3 months test. That choice is important in this domain because attacker wording, domains, infrastructure, and lure formats drift quickly, and the operational question is whether a model trained on historical mail can catch future campaigns. Chronological partitioning also avoids leaking future analyst knowledge and future threat infrastructure into training. Keep all preprocessing fit strictly inside the training block: any scalers, tabular normalizers, categorical vocabularies for engineered features, and URL/domain frequency summaries are learned on training only and then frozen. The transformer may use a fixed pretrained tokenizer, but no statistics from validation or test should inform downstream preprocessing.

The treatment model is a pretrained transformer encoder fine-tuned on raw body text or safe text extracts plus normalized subject and selected header text, combined through a late-fusion head with numeric URL, header, and attachment features. This directly tests the hypothesis that richer sequence representations improve malicious-email detection beyond handcrafted summaries. The baseline is a gradient-boosted tree trained on the standard enterprise feature stack: lexical counts, sender and domain reputation summaries, URL/domain reputation indicators, attachment-type features, and header heuristics. Give both models comparable tuning effort on the validation period so the comparison is about representational power rather than optimization attention.

Use PR-AUC as the primary metric. In a heavily imbalanced gateway setting, security operations care about whether truly harmful emails stay near the top of the score distribution so they can be blocked, quarantined, or escalated before benign traffic creates excess friction. PR-AUC captures how well the model concentrates malicious messages into the actionable scoring region. Also report false-negative rate at the review-capacity operating point, precision and recall at fixed alert-volume budgets, ROC-AUC, and calibration diagnostics, since deployment still depends on choosing stable thresholds.

Perform model selection and early stopping on the validation block only, then lock the pipeline and touch the test period once at the end. To address confounds, provide stratified analyses by month, sender type, and campaign family, and run a sensitivity analysis after deduplicating near-identical resends so any observed gain is not driven solely by one campaign’s repeated templates. This experiment establishes whether the transformer generalizes better to future inbound threats in this organization; it does not by itself prove transferability to other enterprises with different mail mixes or threat exposure.

required_empirical_test.supports_defense_if: Transformer shows ≥3% higher precision at the operational alert volume threshold than the baseline model, validating that the PR-AUC gain reflects improved threshold performance.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: Transformer shows ≥3% lower precision at the operational alert volume threshold than the baseline model, confirming that PR-AUC superiority does not translate to real-world threshold performance.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: Transformer shows ≥3% higher precision at the operational alert volume threshold than the baseline model, validating that the PR-AUC gain reflects improved threshold performance.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: Transformer's precision difference is within ±3 percentage points at the operational threshold — direction of PR-AUC advantage does not strongly predict threshold behavior.

must_not_claim_details.claim: The chronological split method sacrifices statistical power by using only a single train-test partition.
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: Chronological splitting reflects operational reality and ensures temporal generalization in a domain where threat patterns drift rapidly; cross-validation would invalidate the evaluation by allowing future threat patterns into training.

must_not_claim_details.claim: Excluding unresolved cases during label definition introduces selection bias toward easier-to-detect attacks.
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: A 30-day maturation window ensures confident labels by excluding cases still under investigation; including uncertain labels would create false negatives and bias performance metrics.

resolution_descriptions.empirical_test_agreed: Passing verdict — debate recognized the ambiguity and specified an empirical test to resolve it

sound_design_reference:
Use a retrospective but deployment-faithful evaluation on 18 months of inbound mail-gateway traffic from the enterprise environment where the model would be used. Construct the dataset from externally originated inbound messages and retain safe text extracts, subject lines, key headers, URL/domain indicators, attachment metadata, and downstream SOC verdicts. Because labels arrive late, freeze labels only after a 30-day maturation period; messages still under investigation at freeze time should be excluded rather than forced into the negative class. Positives are analyst-confirmed phishing or malicious emails. Negatives are messages with no malicious disposition or incident linkage after maturation.

Split the data chronologically by receipt time: 12 months train, 3 months validation, 3 months test. That choice is important in this domain because attacker wording, domains, infrastructure, and lure formats drift quickly, and the operational question is whether a model trained on historical mail can catch future campaigns. Chronological partitioning also avoids leaking future analyst knowledge and future threat infrastructure into training. Keep all preprocessing fit strictly inside the training block: any scalers, tabular normalizers, categorical vocabularies for engineered features, and URL/domain frequency summaries are learned on training only and then frozen. The transformer may use a fixed pretrained tokenizer, but no statistics from validation or test should inform downstream preprocessing.

The treatment model is a pretrained transformer encoder fine-tuned on raw body text or safe text extracts plus normalized subject and selected header text, combined through a late-fusion head with numeric URL, header, and attachment features. This directly tests the hypothesis that richer sequence representations improve malicious-email detection beyond handcrafted summaries. The baseline is a gradient-boosted tree trained on the standard enterprise feature stack: lexical counts, sender and domain reputation summaries, URL/domain reputation indicators, attachment-type features, and header heuristics. Give both models comparable tuning effort on the validation period so the comparison is about representational power rather than optimization attention.

Use PR-AUC as the primary metric. In a heavily imbalanced gateway setting, security operations care about whether truly harmful emails stay near the top of the score distribution so they can be blocked, quarantined, or escalated before benign traffic creates excess friction. PR-AUC captures how well the model concentrates malicious messages into the actionable scoring region. Also report false-negative rate at the review-capacity operating point, precision and recall at fixed alert-volume budgets, ROC-AUC, and calibration diagnostics, since deployment still depends on choosing stable thresholds.

Perform model selection and early stopping on the validation block only, then lock the pipeline and touch the test period once at the end. To address confounds, provide stratified analyses by month, sender type, and campaign family, and run a sensitivity analysis after deduplicating near-identical resends so any observed gain is not driven solely by one campaign’s repeated templates. This experiment establishes whether the transformer generalizes better to future inbound threats in this organization; it does not by itself prove transferability to other enterprises with different mail mixes or threat exposure.
difficulty_justification: The ambiguous choice centers on primary_metric selection, which requires understanding that the operational deployment is governed by fixed threshold decisions tied to analyst capacity. While PR-AUC is justified as a summary metric, a critic could reasonably argue that threshold-specific behavior determines real-world utility. This can be identified through methodological analysis of the problem domain's constraints without requiring deep cybersecurity expertise.

hypothesis: For enterprise cybersecurity SOC alert triage, a learning-to-rank model that combines SIEM alert features with incident response ticket text and historical analyst feedback will surface true high-priority incidents above the fold more effectively than a classifier-based alert scoring baseline trained only on structured alert metadata.
domain: Cybersecurity operations center (SOC) alert triage for enterprise network intrusion detection
task_prompt:
You would evaluate the hypothesis on production SOC alert streams joined to downstream incident response records over an 18-month period. The unit of prediction is the alert at triage time, but training and evaluation are done as grouped ranking problems so the model learns which alerts should rise within the set actually competing for analyst attention. To keep the study aligned with deployment, include only production alert types and queues that feed the analyst workflow you intend to support.

Labels should reflect downstream operational consequence rather than raw analyst queue order. A practical graded relevance target is 2 for alerts tied to confirmed High/Critical incidents, malicious findings, or containment/remediation within 7 days; 1 for confirmed Medium-severity incidents; and 0 for benign, duplicate, or non-actionable alerts. Cases unresolved after 7 days should be dropped from supervised training because they do not provide stable supervision. This gives a ranking target that prioritizes the incidents the SOC most wants surfaced immediately.

Because attacker behavior, rule tuning, and asset inventory shift over time, use a strict chronological split: 12 months train, 3 months validation, 3 months test. Build ranking groups within analyst queue-day and business unit so each query approximates a real triage slate. All transforms are fit on the training window only. Feature generation must be time-safe: numerical aggregates are computed from history strictly before the alert timestamp, and any text used from tickets or case summaries is limited to text available within two hours of alert creation. Final disposition fields, closure codes, post-incident writeups, and explicit queue-order signals are excluded because they would leak outcomes or simply reproduce prior human decisions.

The proposed model is LambdaMART, using structured SIEM/EDR metadata, short-horizon alert history, entity recurrence statistics, asset/user context, MITRE mappings, and sparse text features from early ticket content. Historical analyst feedback is included only as prior-pattern summaries for rule-asset-user combinations, never as the current alert's own outcome fields. The main baseline is a gradient-boosted classifier trained only on structured metadata and ranked by predicted probability within the same queue groups. This baseline is the correct comparison for the hypothesis because it isolates both the ranking formulation and the added information source from a realistic metadata-only scoring system.

Use NDCG@10 as the primary metric, with MRR, Recall@10, Precision@10, and AUCPR on a binary severe-incident label as secondary metrics. Model selection is performed on the validation window using NDCG@10 with grouped bootstrap intervals over queue-days, and the test window is held untouched until the end. Finally, run ablations by feature family and subgroup checks by business unit, alert family, and unseen rule IDs to determine whether gains are broad or concentrated. The experiment can establish improved historical triage prioritization under drift-aware evaluation; it does not by itself prove causal reduction in dwell time or immunity to policy-driven label bias.

ground_truth.final_verdict: Empirically contested — the design choice at issue cannot be validated without measurement

required_empirical_test.condition: Compute the operational review cutoff K as the median number of alerts reviewed per analyst queue within the first 30 minutes of triage. Compare the candidate and baseline models' NDCG@10 and Precision@K scores on the validation set: (1) Check whether the relative model order flips between metrics (e.g., model A > model B in NDCG@10 but model B > model A in Precision@K), or (2) Check whether absolute Precision@K gap exceeds 0.05 while NDCG@10 difference is less than 0.01.
required_empirical_test.supports_critique_if: The models’ relative performance differs between NDCG@10 and Precision@K or the Precision@K gap exceeds 0.05 when NDCG@10 difference is below 0.01
required_empirical_test.supports_defense_if: The models' orderings are consistent across metrics and the Precision@K gap does not exceed 0.05 when NDCG@10 differs by less than 0.01

ideal_debate_resolution.condition: Compute the operational review cutoff K as the median number of alerts reviewed per analyst queue within the first 30 minutes of triage. Compare the candidate and baseline models' NDCG@10 and Precision@K scores on the validation set: (1) Check whether the relative model order flips between metrics (e.g., model A > model B in NDCG@10 but model B > model A in Precision@K), or (2) Check whether absolute Precision@K gap exceeds 0.05 while NDCG@10 difference is less than 0.01.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: The models’ relative performance differs between NDCG@10 and Precision@K or the Precision@K gap exceeds 0.05 when NDCG@10 difference is below 0.01
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: The models' orderings are consistent across metrics and the Precision@K gap does not exceed 0.05 when NDCG@10 differs by less than 0.01

must_not_claim_details.claim: Strict chronological splitting reduces training data diversity and harms generalization
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: Cybersecurity data is temporally non-stationary due to attacker behavior evolution and detector reconfiguration; chronological splits validate robustness under operational drift, which is the intended use case

must_not_claim_details.claim: Excluding unresolved alerts creates a biased dataset missing edge cases
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: Unresolved alerts have ambiguous labels that could encode prior analyst decisions rather than incident severity; exclusion ensures supervision reflects actionable, stable ground truth aligned with response outcomes
