hypothesis: For enterprise cybersecurity vulnerability management, a learning-to-rank model that combines asset inventory, network topology, and scanner findings will identify the subset of vulnerabilities most likely to be remediated within 14 days better than a baseline ranked only by severity score (e.g., CVSS), as measured by top-k precision on historical remediation actions.
domain: Cybersecurity vulnerability management for enterprise endpoint and server fleets using asset inventory, network topology, and vulnerability scanner data
task_prompt:
You would evaluate the hypothesis on an 18-month extract from the enterprise vulnerability scanner, CMDB, network exposure data, and remediation ticketing/patch records. The prediction target is defined at the moment a vulnerability finding first appears on an asset after being absent on the prior clean scan. Each example is labeled positive when the finding is remediated within 14 days, verified through ticket closure plus either scanner disappearance or a patch event. Instances lacking a full 14-day observation window are dropped so the label is well defined.

The experiment uses a single decision-time snapshot per finding instance rather than every repeated scan row. This keeps the unit of prediction aligned with how analysts actually prioritize work and avoids inflating sample size with near-duplicate observations. Train, validation, and test sets are created with a stratified random split over finding instances, preserving the overall 14-day remediation rate and maintaining similar mixes of business unit and asset class across splits. Leakage is controlled by excluding any feature updated after ranking time, including later ticket states, subsequent scan results, and post hoc patch metadata.

Feature construction combines the operational signals security teams already use with structural context they usually do not. Vulnerability-level features include CVSS components, exploitability flags, vulnerability age, recurrence on the same asset, and patch availability. Asset-level features include criticality, platform, role, maintenance window, ownership, and backlog size. Topology features summarize network exposure and reachability, such as internet exposure, count of externally reachable paths, subnet centrality, and lateral-movement adjacency counts. Historical team throughput features are computed only from data available prior to the ranking date. All preprocessing, including imputation and encoding, is fit on training data only, then frozen for validation and test.

The proposed model is LambdaMART, trained as a learning-to-rank system over weekly remediation queues. That is the right model class for the hypothesis because the question is not whether an individual finding will eventually be fixed, but whether the model can order findings so the likely-to-be-remediated items rise to the top of each team’s actionable queue. The primary baseline is the current operational heuristic: rank by CVSS severity, breaking ties with exploitability and asset criticality. A stronger baseline is a tuned logistic-regression model using the same standard tabular signals but excluding topology-derived features; this tests whether the ranking lift comes specifically from richer context rather than simply moving beyond CVSS.

Model selection is performed only on the validation set using mean Precision@K, where K matches each team’s typical weekly remediation capacity and results are macro-averaged across queues. Secondary reporting includes NDCG@K, MAP@K, and AUCPR. The test set is used once at the end, with clustered bootstrap intervals by asset and queue to account for repeated structure. To address the main confound, you would run sensitivity analyses by owner team and business unit, plus a leave-one-team-out check and an ablation removing ownership features, so the study can distinguish genuine prioritization signal from simply learning which teams patch fastest.

ground_truth.final_verdict: Empirically contested — the design choice at issue cannot be validated without measurement

required_empirical_test.condition: Compute the autocorrelation of the daily 14-day remediation rate time series at a 14-day lag on the pre-split dataset. If the lag-14 autocorrelation coefficient exceeds 0.30, chronological splitting is required; if 0.30 or below, stratified random splitting is acceptable.
required_empirical_test.supports_critique_if: If the lag-14 autocorrelation coefficient is greater than 0.30, indicating substantial temporal dependence that would allow leakage of operational patterns from the future into training data.
required_empirical_test.supports_defense_if: If the lag-14 autocorrelation coefficient is 0.30 or below, suggesting the remediation patterns are sufficiently temporally decorrelated to justify random splitting.

ideal_debate_resolution.condition: Compute the autocorrelation of the daily 14-day remediation rate time series at a 14-day lag on the pre-split dataset. If the lag-14 autocorrelation coefficient exceeds 0.30, chronological splitting is required; if 0.30 or below, stratified random splitting is acceptable.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: If the lag-14 autocorrelation coefficient is greater than 0.30, indicating substantial temporal dependence that would allow leakage of operational patterns from the future into training data.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: If the lag-14 autocorrelation coefficient is 0.30 or below, suggesting the remediation patterns are sufficiently temporally decorrelated to justify random splitting.

must_not_claim_details.claim: Excluding vulnerability instances without 14 days of follow-up introduces selection bias by discarding valuable partial data
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: The hypothesis requires a clearly defined binary label (remediated/delayed) to train a ranking system. Including partial observations would require interval-censored modeling or complex time-to-event methods not aligned with the operational goal of predicting actionable remediation probabilities at discovery time.

must_not_claim_details.claim: Collapsing repeated scan rows into a single instance removes time-series signals critical for modeling dynamic remediation behavior
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: The experiment's unit of decision is the vulnerability's first appearance, mirroring how analysts prioritize newly discovered findings. Retaining repeated rows would artificially inflate observations without adding new remediation decisions, while time-series dynamics beyond the discovery-time snapshot are explicitly excluded to prevent leakage of post-ranking information.

hypothesis: For enterprise software repositories, a model that combines commit diffs, code-review comments, and dependency-change graphs will detect supply-chain security vulnerabilities better than a baseline trained only on static repository metadata, as measured by AUROC on future vulnerable commits from later time periods.
domain: Software supply-chain security analytics for enterprise code repositories and dependency management systems
task_prompt:
You would frame the experiment around the actual decision point: scoring a commit or pull request before release using only information available at that time. Assemble four years of timestamped repository history, PR and review logs, dependency manifests and lockfiles, dependency-resolution graphs reconstructed per change, CI scan outputs, and confirmed security findings. The unit of prediction should be the commit or PR that introduced the change. A positive label is assigned when that change is later confirmed as the introduction point for a supply-chain security issue. Because security labels arrive late, impose a 90-day maturation window and drop changes too recent to have had a realistic chance of being discovered; this avoids contaminating negatives with unresolved incidents.

Use a strict chronological split: the earliest 70% of matured examples for training, the next 15% for validation, and the final 15% for test. Keep all commits belonging to the same PR together. This split is necessary because the deployment question is explicitly about future vulnerable changes, and both labels and ecosystem state evolve over time. Any preprocessing artifact that learns from data distribution—text vocabularies, imputers, scaling constants, category maps, feature selection thresholds—must be fit on training only and then frozen.

Construct two model conditions on the identical example set. The baseline uses only static repository metadata and operational context: repository age, language mix, author/reviewer counts, churn, paths touched, historical build status, dependency counts, package ecosystem, and prior alert history. The proposed model adds three signal families: commit diff features, code-review comment features, and dependency-change graph features. For commit diffs and review comments, derive compact text embeddings plus interpretable statistics such as token risk lexicons, edit size, and discussion intensity. For dependency graphs, compute features tied to supply-chain exposure: new package introductions, transitive dependency reachability, centrality shifts, maintainer concentration proxies, version-jump magnitude, and known-risk indicators available at decision time. Feed all modalities into a late-fusion gradient-boosted tree classifier so interactions among metadata, text, and graph signals can be learned without giving the richer model an unfair tuning advantage.

Tune both baseline and full model with the same validation-set search budget and class-weighting strategy. Select the best configuration by validation AUROC, then evaluate once on the untouched future test period. AUROC is the primary metric because the operational workflow is score-based triage: different business units review different fractions of incoming changes, so the model must preserve a useful risk ordering across a range of queue capacities rather than optimize only a single cutoff. Because the data are imbalanced, also report AUPRC, recall at top-k review budgets, precision at the current team capacity threshold, and calibration via Brier score.

To address confounds, include repository identity and age signals in both models, report per-repository and per-ecosystem breakdowns, and run an additional repository-held-out analysis. The experiment establishes whether multi-modal change information improves future vulnerability ranking beyond metadata alone; it does not by itself prove causal mechanisms or guarantee transfer to organizations with substantially different review practices or package ecosystems.

ground_truth.final_verdict: Empirically contested — the design choice at issue cannot be validated without measurement

required_empirical_test.condition: Compute precision and recall at the historical median review queue budget (e.g., top 5% of daily scored changes) for both models on the test set. Compare the full model's precision and recall at this threshold against the baseline. If the full model's precision at the threshold is less than 2 percentage points higher than the baseline and its recall gain is less than 5 percentage points, then AUROC alone is insufficient as the primary metric.
required_empirical_test.supports_critique_if: The full model's precision at the operational threshold is not at least 2 percentage points higher than the baseline AND its recall gain is less than 5 percentage points
required_empirical_test.supports_defense_if: The full model's precision at the operational threshold is at least 2 percentage points higher than the baseline OR its recall gain is at least 5 percentage points

ideal_debate_resolution.condition: Compute precision and recall at the historical median review queue budget (e.g., top 5% of daily scored changes) for both models on the test set. Compare the full model's precision and recall at this threshold against the baseline. If the full model's precision at the threshold is less than 2 percentage points higher than the baseline and its recall gain is less than 5 percentage points, then AUROC alone is insufficient as the primary metric.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: The full model's precision at the operational threshold is at least 2 percentage points higher than the baseline OR its recall gain is at least 5 percentage points

must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: The metadata-only baseline isolates the incremental value of commit, review, and graph signals. Using the same model family and tuning budget ensures methodological parity, avoiding attribution confusions

must_not_claim_details.claim: Late-fusion gradient-boosted trees are outdated when deep learning architectures would better capture cross-modal interactions
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: Late-fusion trees with frozen preprocessing ensure methodological transparency. The choice avoids confounding model family differences with feature-set value, enabling cleaner attribution of gains

hypothesis: For enterprise cybersecurity SOC alert triage, a learning-to-rank model that uses the full alert chain and incident-response context will rank true high-priority incidents above routine alerts better than a binary classifier trained on the same features when evaluated on held-out future weeks of analyst-reviewed alerts.
domain: Cybersecurity operations center (SOC) alert triage for enterprise network intrusion detection
task_prompt:
You would frame the experiment around the actual SOC decision point: an alert arrives, the system has access to alert-native fields plus contemporaneous context, and the goal is to order the queue so analysts see the most dangerous work first. Use 18 months of SIEM/EDR alerts linked to case-management tickets, enrichment records, and asset/user metadata. Every example is anchored at alert creation time, and any field first populated after analyst action begins is excluded. That includes containment actions, analyst free text, final ticket severity fields, and late enrichment artifacts that would reveal the investigation outcome.

Construct alert-level examples because the operational queue is alert driven, but derive graded relevance from the eventual incident outcome. Alerts tied to confirmed malicious incidents with final high or critical severity receive the highest gain, escalated but lower-severity cases receive intermediate gain, and routine or benign alerts receive zero. Keep duplicate alerts as separate queue items because they compete for analyst attention in production, and add a secondary incident-deduplicated analysis to ensure results are not just driven by repeated copies of the same event chain.

Split data chronologically by week: 12 months train, 3 months validation, 3 months test. This is the right deployment-faithful split for a SOC setting because both attacker behavior and internal detection content evolve over time, and later weeks contain enrichment patterns and workflow conventions that should not inform model development. Fit all preprocessors on training only. For categorical fields, collapse rare levels based on training frequency; for numeric fields, impute and scale from training statistics only; for any text-derived detector metadata, build the vocabulary on training only.

The treatment model is a gradient-boosted learning-to-rank system trained with groupwise ranking by analyst queue week and optimized on graded relevance. Grouping by week reflects how analysts consume a rolling queue and prevents the model from learning a ranking objective over arbitrary global mixes of alerts that never co-occur operationally. The baseline is deliberately strong: a gradient-boosted binary classifier using the same features, same preprocessing, and a comparable hyperparameter search budget. Its output probabilities are used to produce a ranked list, so the comparison isolates whether direct ranking optimization adds value beyond standard classification on identical information.

sound_design_reference:

Alerts tied to confirmed malicious incidents with final high or critical severity receive the highest gain, escalated but lower-severity cases receive intermediate gain, and routine or benign alerts receive zero. Keep duplicate alerts as separate queue items because they compete for analyst attention in production, and add a secondary incident-deduplicated analysis to ensure results are not just driven by repeated copies of the same event chain.

Fit all preprocessors on training only. For categorical fields, collapse rare levels based on training frequency; for numeric fields, impute and scale from training statistics only; for any text-derived detector metadata, build the vocabulary on training only.

The treatment model is a gradient-boosted learning-to-rank system trained with groupwise ranking by analyst queue week and optimized on graded relevance. hypothesis: For enterprise email security, a transformer model fine-tuned on raw message text plus header/URL features will detect phishing and malicious email more accurately than a gradient-boosted model trained only on engineered sender-reputation and metadata features, as measured on a temporally held-out month of inbound mail.
domain: Cybersecurity — enterprise email security using inbound message text, headers, URLs, and sender reputation logs
task_prompt:
You would evaluate the hypothesis on six consecutive months of inbound mail from the enterprise gateway, using only information available at receipt time. The dataset should include raw subject and body text, MIME structure, headers, authentication results, URLs, attachment metadata, and sender-reputation logs. Labels are built from confirmed phishing and malicious-email outcomes: analyst triage, validated user reports, and incident-response confirmations define positives. Negatives are messages with no malicious disposition after a 21-day observation window and no later incident linkage. Messages with disputed or unresolved outcomes are dropped rather than forced into a noisy class.

Because email threats drift quickly and campaign artifacts recur over short intervals, the split is chronological by receipt month: months 1-4 for training, month 5 for validation, and month 6 for the final held-out test. This preserves realistic future generalization and avoids leakage from campaign timing. Near-identical parser duplicates are deduplicated within each split so the model is not rewarded for memorizing repeated ingestion artifacts, but cross-month recurrence is preserved because that is part of the deployment reality.

The proposed model is a transformer fine-tuned on raw subject and message body text, with structured header, URL, attachment, and reputation features fused through a small classification head. Preprocessing that learns from data—numeric scaling, imputers, frequency encodings, and any learned categorical mappings—is fit on the training period only and then frozen. Rule-based parsing and normalization, such as URL canonicalization and header extraction, are fixed prior to training. Features that can directly encode the label or future actions, such as analyst queue status, quarantine disposition after review, or downstream user-click outcomes, are excluded.

The baseline is a strong gradient-boosted tree model trained on engineered metadata and reputation features only. It receives comparable tuning effort on the validation month so the comparison tests the value of raw-text representation rather than tuning asymmetry. This is the relevant baseline because many operational mail-security stacks rely primarily on sender reputation, authentication, routing, and structural indicators.

Model selection is performed on month 5 using average precision as the primary metric. In this environment, positives are rare and security operations consume a ranked stream of suspicious mail; what matters is how concentrated the true malicious messages are near the top of that ranking so review burden stays low while broad detection remains high. Secondary reporting includes precision at fixed recall, recall at high-precision operating points, AUROC, calibration diagnostics, and work saved over sampling.

The test month is touched once after architecture, tuning, and calibration are locked. Results should be reported with both message-level and campaign-cluster bootstrap intervals. To address confounds, include subgroup analyses for novel senders versus previously seen senders, label-source strata, and campaign clusters, plus an ablation that removes raw text from the transformer pathway. This experiment can show whether the multimodal transformer generalizes better on a future month of this organization’s mail stream; it will not by itself prove superiority for every deployment policy or every enterprise environment.

ground_truth.final_verdict: Empirically contested — the design choice at issue cannot be validated without measurement

required_empirical_test.supports_defense_if: If the precision difference (transformer - baseline) is ≥3 percentage points, confirming average precision aligns with threshold-specific operational needs.

ideal_debate_resolution.condition: On the held-out month-6 test set, measure precision at recall=0.85 for both models. Compute the difference between transformer and gradient-boosted models (transformer_precision - baseline_precision).
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: If the precision difference (transformer - baseline) is less than 3 percentage points or negative, indicating the average-precision-optimized model underperforms at the operational recall target.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: If the precision difference (transformer - baseline) is ≥3 percentage points, confirming average precision aligns with threshold-specific operational needs.

hypothesis: A gradient-boosted model using sequence features from source code changes, pull-request metadata, and developer collaboration graph signals will predict post-merge production incidents better than a baseline trained only on engineered repository-level metrics for enterprise DevOps teams, as measured by out-of-time AUROC and precision at the top risk decile.
domain: Software engineering / DevOps analytics for enterprise code repositories and pull requests
task_prompt:
Use merged pull requests as the prediction unit and construct a 24-month historical dataset from source-control, code-review, CI, deployment, and incident systems for repositories with reliable merge-to-deploy linkage. Define the label as whether the deployed PR is associated with a production incident or rollback within 7 days of first production deployment. Because attribution is imperfect, keep the labeling rule operationally strict and consistent: only incidents tied through deployment or rollback records are positive. Exclude PRs lacking deployment traceability from supervised training, but report how much traffic this removes to quantify coverage bias.

Respect the production setting with a chronological split by merge date: train on the earliest 70% of the timeline, tune on the next 15%, and reserve the final 15% as a pure out-of-time test. This matches the stated goal of forecasting future risky changes under process drift. Leakage is controlled by computing every feature from information available no later than the PR merge timestamp and by enforcing the 7-day outcome window before assigning labels. Encoders, imputers, and any normalization statistics are fit only on training data.

Engineer three feature families. First, sequence features summarize the dynamics of change creation and review: number of commits, elapsed time between commits, churn trajectories, touched-file entropy, reviewer turnaround, comment-thread depth, CI rerun sequences, and failure-to-pass patterns. Second, PR and repository context features capture static and recent operational state: changed files, added/deleted lines, language mix, service ownership, historical deploy cadence, rolling incident rate, branch age, and CI stability. Third, developer collaboration graph features capture organization structure at merge time: author-reviewer centrality, prior collaboration counts, cross-team review edges, reviewer-set novelty, and bus-factor proxies. Exclude any feature generated after merge or derived from incident systems except lagged historical aggregates.

Train a LightGBM classifier on the full feature set with class weighting and early stopping. Tune depth, leaves, learning rate, minimum leaf size, and subsampling on the validation block. Compare it against a fair baseline of the same model family trained only on repository-level engineered metrics. Using the same learner and tuning budget isolates the value of sequence and collaboration signals rather than conflating feature improvements with a more expressive algorithm.

Use AUROC on the out-of-time test set as the primary metric. The deployment use case is score-based triage: engineering leaders need a stable ranking of merged changes so scarce additional review, canarying, or staged rollout capacity can be directed toward the riskiest items across many teams. Since incident prevalence is low, also report precision and recall at the top risk decile, average precision, and Brier score. Model selection is driven by validation AUROC, with a check that top-decile precision is not materially degraded.

Control for confounds by excluding raw repository and team identifiers, using lagged and smoothed historical-rate features, and reporting subgroup performance by repository criticality, business unit, and incident prevalence. The experiment establishes whether richer code-change and collaboration signals improve incident-risk prediction beyond historical repository context under realistic forward-looking evaluation; it does not establish causal mechanisms for why individual changes fail in production.

hypothesis: For enterprise cybersecurity vulnerability management, a learning-to-rank model that combines asset inventory, network topology, and vulnerability scanner data will surface the vulnerabilities most likely to be exploited within 30 days better than a severity-only ranking based on CVSS scores.
domain: Cybersecurity vulnerability management for enterprise endpoint and server fleets using asset inventory, network topology, and vulnerability scanner data
task_prompt:
To test the hypothesis, build a retrospective ranking dataset from 12 months of vulnerability management operations data. Each example is an asset-vulnerability finding at the time it first appears in a scanner snapshot. Join scanner output to asset inventory, CMDB criticality, exposure metadata, and snapshot-consistent network topology summaries. Define the outcome on a 30-day forward window: a finding is positive if the same vulnerability on the same asset is later confirmed exploited or linked to an incident within 30 days through EDR, SIEM, threat hunting, or incident-response records. Restrict the study to assets with stable telemetry and scanner coverage so negatives are not dominated by simple observability gaps.

Use a chronological split by observation week: 70% train, 15% validation, 15% test. This is the correct deployment-faithful split because ranking decisions are made on newly observed findings, and any random interleaving of weeks would let the model absorb future remediation behavior, incident investigation intensity, or changing scanner policy. All preprocessing is fit on training only. Imputation, categorical encoding, and any scaling are learned on the training period and then applied unchanged to validation and test.

Engineer features from four sources: vulnerability attributes, asset context, finding state, and network topology. Include CVSS components, scanner severity, vulnerability age, exploit-publication flags if available, asset OS and role, business criticality, external exposure, prior patch latency by asset class, local vulnerability density, and graph summaries such as connectivity, segment exposure, and neighborhood risk concentration. Exclude any feature created after ranking time, especially remediation ticket status, analyst annotations, and incident workflow fields.

Train a LambdaMART learning-to-rank model with observation week as the ranking group. This matches the operational problem: the security team reviews a bounded weekly queue and needs the highest-risk findings surfaced first. Compare against a severity-only CVSS ranking, which is the practical incumbent, and against a logistic regression baseline limited to CVSS-derived vulnerability fields so the comparison is not merely “model versus heuristic” but “enterprise context versus severity-only information.” Give both learned models equivalent validation-based tuning effort.

Select hyperparameters on the validation period using mean NDCG@100 across weeks, with early stopping and a modest search over tree depth, learning rate, and leaf size. NDCG@100 is the primary metric because remediation capacity is finite and the operational question is whether the top review set contains the findings most likely to be exploited soon. Also report Precision@25, Recall@100, MAP, and AUCPR for supplementary visibility into extreme-top performance and class imbalance.

Hold the test set untouched until the end, then report final performance with bootstrap confidence intervals over weeks. To address confounds, repeat evaluation within internet-exposed vs internal assets, high- vs lower-criticality assets, and servers vs endpoints, and run feature-family ablations for recency, exposure, and criticality. This experiment will show whether adding enterprise context improves prioritization beyond CVSS-centric ranking under realistic operational constraints.

hypothesis: In enterprise cybersecurity vulnerability management, a ranking model that incorporates asset graph features and recent exploit intelligence will prioritize truly high-risk endpoints more accurately than a model using scanner severity scores alone, improving the top-k capture of critical vulnerabilities within a 7-day remediation window.
domain: Cybersecurity vulnerability management for enterprise endpoint and server fleets using asset inventory, network topology, and vulnerability scanner data
task_prompt:
You would evaluate the hypothesis on a longitudinal weekly panel of asset-vulnerability observations built from scanner output, inventory, patching records, network topology, and exploit-intelligence feeds over an 18-month period. Each record corresponds to an open vulnerability on an asset at a given scan week. Repeated detections within the same week are deduplicated, and open findings persist until remediation is observed. To avoid training on poorly observed systems, include only endpoints and servers with at least 60 days of prior inventory visibility.

The target should reflect what the remediation team actually cares about: exposures that become urgent within the next 7 days. Label an asset-vulnerability pair positive if it is tied to a confirmed incident on that asset during the window or if it remains unremediated past the organization’s critical-vulnerability SLA while active exploit intelligence is present. This preserves operational relevance while avoiding a label defined purely by patching convenience.

Use a strict chronological split: 12 months train, 3 months validation, 3 months test. In this setting, exploit campaigns, patch behavior, and asset criticality drift materially over time, so the evaluation needs to mirror prospective prioritization. All transformations are fit on training data only. Graph features are recomputed week by week from the network snapshot available at that time, and no post-ranking remediation or incident annotations are allowed into features.

Engineer features from four sources: scanner-native severity and finding history, asset business context, recent exploit intelligence, and graph structure. Useful graph features include degree, approximate betweenness, reachability to internet-exposed nodes, proximity to crown-jewel assets, and local concentration of similar findings. Also include controls for scan cadence and historical remediation latency so the model is not rewarded simply for learning which systems are looked at often.

Train a LambdaMART ranker on weekly candidate sets. Compare it against two baselines: the organization’s current heuristic ranking based on scanner severity, exploit presence, and finding age, and a linear learning-to-rank model trained on the same non-graph engineered features with a comparable tuning budget. This gives both an operational baseline and a fair learned baseline.

Select models using weekly mean NDCG@500 on the validation period. NDCG@500 is the primary metric because remediation teams work through a bounded queue each week, and the main question is whether the most dangerous exposures are moved upward within that actionable slice. Report Recall@100, Recall@500, Precision@100, MRR, and AUCPR as secondary diagnostics. Once hyperparameters and feature sets are fixed, retrain on train plus validation and evaluate once on the held-out test period.

To address confounds, run subgroup checks by asset type, business unit, and internet exposure, and verify that improvements persist after controlling for recency, scan frequency, and team-level patching effects. The result would establish whether graph and exploit-intelligence features improve prioritization quality for this enterprise remediation workflow; it would not by itself prove causal exploit prediction outside this environment.

required_empirical_test.condition: Measure the organization's actual weekly vulnerability remediation capacity over the 12 weeks preceding model deployment to determine the median K findings reviewed per week. Recompute model validation rankings by truncating at K and compare Precision@K and Recall@K against the existing NDCG@500 metric. A winning model under NDCG@500 must show that its Precision@K does not trail the top-performing model by more than 5 percentage points at the cutoff.
required_empirical_test.supports_critique_if: The current winning model under NDCG@500 scores 5 percentage points lower in Precision@K than the top-performing model at the determined cutoff K.
required_empirical_test.supports_defense_if: The current winning model under NDCG@500 scores within 5 percentage points or higher in Precision@K compared to all other evaluated models at the determined cutoff K.
required_empirical_test.ambiguous_if: If the Precision@K difference between the NDCG@500-selected model and the top-performing model at cutoff K is exactly ±5 percentage points.

A winning model under NDCG@500 must show that its Precision@K does not trail the top-performing model by more than 5 percentage points at the cutoff.

hypothesis: For enterprise cybersecurity alert triage, a ranking model that combines SIEM/EDR alert features with incident response ticket text and historical analyst dispositions will surface true incidents higher in the queue than a feature-only gradient-boosted classifier, improving top-k precision and reducing mean time to investigation.
domain: Cybersecurity operations center (SOC) alert triage using SIEM alerts, endpoint telemetry, and incident response tickets from a mid-to-large enterprise
task_prompt:
You would evaluate the hypothesis on two years of enterprise SOC data integrating SIEM alerts, EDR telemetry, and case-management records. The prediction unit is the individual alert, but all alerts linked to the same investigation case are treated as a dependency group throughout the pipeline. Ground-truth relevance is defined by downstream incident confirmation: alerts tied to cases that end in a confirmed security incident, or that are escalated into a confirmed incident workflow within seven days, are relevant. Alerts with unresolved final status are excluded from supervised training so the target reflects operationally meaningful incidents rather than analyst uncertainty.

Use a chronological split by alert creation time, with case-level grouping to prevent leakage across partitions. Train on months 1-16, validate on months 17-20, and test on months 21-24. This mirrors the real deployment problem: the model ranks incoming alerts using only information available before those alerts arrive. It also avoids contamination from repeated cases, reopened investigations, or ticket-thread artifacts that could otherwise let the model memorize outcomes across splits.

Feature construction should combine three sources of signal. First, build structured alert features from SIEM/EDR data: rule name, product, severity, tactic/technique tags, host criticality, user risk tier, entity counts, and rolling counts of related alerts in trailing windows. Second, encode linked ticket text that is available at ranking time using TF-IDF n-grams plus a compact semantic embedding, capturing analyst-described context that does not exist in raw telemetry alone. Third, add historical analyst disposition statistics, such as prior escalation rates for the same rule, host class, or user segment, computed strictly from events preceding the alert timestamp. Exclude closure codes, final disposition fields, post-triage comments, and any timestamps created after the initial queueing event.

Train a LambdaMART ranking model over daily operational queue groups, using the full structured-plus-text feature set. Compare it against a feature-only gradient-boosted classifier trained on the same alert and telemetry features but without ticket text or historical disposition context. Give both approaches comparable tuning effort on the validation period so the experiment tests whether richer context improves prioritization, not whether one model received more optimization.

Use NDCG@20 as the primary metric because analysts act on a ranked queue and most operational value is concentrated in the first screenful of alerts; the metric rewards getting true incidents near the top while preserving sensitivity to ordering within those high-value positions. Also report Precision@10, Precision@20, MRR, and a test-period replay estimate of mean time to first investigation. Hyperparameters are selected on the validation window only, with grouped cross-validation inside the training period for coarse search. The test set is touched once at the end, and uncertainty is estimated by bootstrapping case-grouped ranking units.

To address confounds, include alert age, native severity, and source system in all models; report sliced performance by source and severity; and run an ablation removing ticket metadata fields that could simply encode analyst workflow shortcuts. This design establishes whether richer contextual ranking improves operational prioritization under realistic SOC conditions, but it does not by itself prove causal reduction in incident risk outside the measured queueing workflow.

required_empirical_test.condition: Compare the candidate model and baseline model's Precision@10 and NDCG@20 on the held-out test set. For alerts grouped by operational queue day, compute: (1) NDCG@20, (2) Precision@10 where K=10 reflects analysts' historical first-screen review depth. Annotate which alerts analysts reviewed and flagged during deployment if real-world feedback is available.
required_empirical_test.supports_critique_if: Candidate model's Precision@10 is ≥2 percentage points lower than baseline while NDCG@20 is higher (ΔNDCG>0.03 MRR-equivalent improvement).
required_empirical_test.supports_defense_if: Candidate model's Precision@10 is <2 percentage points lower than baseline while NDCG@20 is higher, or Precision@10 improves.

ideal_debate_resolution.condition: Compare the candidate model and baseline model's Precision@10 and NDCG@20 on the held-out test set. ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: Candidate model's Precision@10 is <2 percentage points lower than baseline while NDCG@20 is higher, or Precision@10 improves.

hypothesis: For enterprise cybersecurity alert triage, a learning-to-rank model that jointly uses SIEM alert features, historical incident-response tickets, and analyst disposition feedback will surface true high-severity incidents higher in the queue than a pointwise binary classifier trained only to predict alert maliciousness, as measured by top-k precision and time-to-triage on a held-out future period.
domain: Cybersecurity operations center (SOC) alert triage for enterprise network intrusion detection
task_prompt:
You would evaluate this hypothesis as a future-period ranking problem anchored to the SOC’s actual queueing workflow. Start from 18 months of SIEM and EDR alerts and join each alert to entity context, historical incident-response tickets, analyst dispositions, escalations, and final incident severities. Because attacker behavior, detection content, and analyst practices drift over time, divide the data chronologically into 12 months for training, 3 months for validation, and 3 months for test. Every feature must be computed as-of the alert timestamp, and preprocessing artifacts such as imputation values, categorical vocabularies, and scaling parameters are fit only on training data.

Define the supervision target at the alert level using downstream operational outcomes rather than raw alert fire/no-fire status. An alert is positive if it is linked within 7 days to a Sev1/Sev2 incident ticket or an analyst-confirmed malicious disposition when no ticket exists. Benign closures are negative. Because labels are delayed and many alerts never mature to a definitive outcome, apply a 14-day maturation requirement before an example is eligible for supervised training; unresolved alerts are excluded from model fitting to reduce noise from incomplete feedback.

Construct three feature blocks: native alert features from the SIEM/EDR event and rule metadata; entity-centered behavioral aggregates over the prior 30 days; and historical analyst feedback features such as prior malicious disposition rates and escalation propensity for similar rule/entity cohorts. The 30-day lookback is a practical fit for SOC operations because most repeated scanning, beaconing, credential abuse bursts, and recurring rule/entity interactions that influence triage urgency occur on short operational cycles, while keeping the feature store compact and straightforward to recompute in production. Exclude any post-alert artifacts such as ticket severity, closure notes, or enrichments added only after review begins.

Train the proposed model as a LambdaMART learning-to-rank system, grouping alerts into hourly ingestion batches or analyst queue slices so the objective matches the within-queue ordering decision analysts actually face. The baseline should be a pointwise gradient-boosted binary classifier trained on the exact same features, labels, temporal split, and tuning budget. That comparison is the right test of the hypothesis because it holds data richness constant and isolates whether the ranking formulation improves prioritization.

Select hyperparameters on the validation period using mean Precision@K across queue slices, where K is set to the median number of alerts analysts can realistically review from a batch during a shift. Precision@K is the primary metric because the operational value comes from what lands near the top of the queue, not from average discrimination over the full alert distribution. Report NDCG@K, Recall@K, PR-AUC, and median simulated time-to-triage for true high-severity incidents as secondary metrics. The test period is touched only once after all choices are frozen, and uncertainty is summarized with bootstrap intervals over queue slices.

This experiment would establish whether ranking on richer joint context improves future-period prioritization under realistic SOC conditions. It would not, by itself, prove causal impact on analyst behavior or eliminate selection bias introduced by historical triage practices, so results should be interpreted as retrospective decision-support evidence rather than a deployment-effect estimate.

required_empirical_test.supports_defense_if: Precision@K improves by <0.02 and NDCG@K improves by <0.03

The evaluation retains these alerts for queue-level denominator analyses where appropriate, preserving their informational role without introducing label instability into model fitting.

hypothesis: For enterprise cybersecurity threat detection, a graph-based model that incorporates user-device-email relationship features will identify account-takeover incidents more accurately than a tabular model trained only on per-event identity provider and device telemetry, as measured on future incidents in a chronologically held-out period.
domain: Cybersecurity threat detection for enterprise account takeover investigation using identity provider logs, device telemetry, and email security events
task_prompt:
You would evaluate the hypothesis as a future-incident binary classification problem at the user-day level. The data should cover 18 months of routine production telemetry: identity provider authentication and SSO logs, endpoint/device events, email security events, and analyst-confirmed account-takeover outcomes. Because ground truth is delayed, labels should be generated only where case closure maturity is sufficient. A positive label is assigned to user-days within the pre-investigation detection window for a confirmed account takeover, anchored to the earliest verified compromise onset when available and otherwise to a conservative backdated proxy from investigation open time. User-days with no linked takeover investigation in the following 30 days serve as negatives. Periods with unresolved investigations are excluded.

The split should be strictly chronological. Train on months 1-12, validate on months 13-15, and test on months 16-18, with a 30-day maturity buffer at each boundary. All features must be computed using only information available up to the scoring timestamp, which avoids leakage from future campaign artifacts, retrospective analyst knowledge, or post-incident enrichment.

To make the comparison fair, both models should use the same raw event sources and the same 30-day cutoff window. The tabular pipeline will build per-user/day aggregates such as failed login counts, MFA challenge patterns, impossible-travel flags, IP risk summaries, geovelocity, new-device indicators, endpoint alert rates, mailbox rule creation, unusual sender/recipient behavior, and deviations from each user’s historical baseline. The graph pipeline will use those same raw signals to form a heterogeneous graph over users, devices, IPs, mailboxes, and message entities, with recency-weighted edges and node attributes derived from the same aggregates. Analyst workflow fields, ticket metadata, and any post-investigation annotations are excluded from both pipelines.

The proposed model is a heterogeneous graph neural network with two message-passing layers and class-weighted loss. The baseline is a gradient-boosted tree trained on the matched tabular feature set. Both receive comparable tuning effort on the validation period so the comparison tests representational advantage rather than optimization budget.

Model selection is done on the validation period using PR-AUC as the primary metric. That metric matches the operational setting because account takeover is rare and the SOC consumes a ranked queue under variable daily capacity; the key question is whether true compromises are concentrated near the top of that queue. Secondary reporting should include precision at the top 0.5% of scored user-days, recall at the historical alert budget, AUROC, and calibration via Brier score. The test set is touched once at the end for final reporting only.

To address confounds, hold raw inputs, label logic, and lookback window constant across models. Report subgroup results by campaign cluster, policy segment, and unseen-device prevalence, and run ablations that remove email-derived features from both systems. This design establishes whether the graph formulation improves forward-looking detection performance under realistic deployment constraints; it does not by itself prove which relational mechanism is responsible for any observed gain.

required_empirical_test.condition: Compute both models' recall at the fixed historical investigation budget and precision at the alert threshold corresponding to the top 0.5% of scored user-days on the validation set. If both differences are within ±3 percentage points, PR-AUC alone is sufficient; if either difference exceeds 3 percentage points in the PR-AUC-favored model's disfavor, PR-AUC must be demoted and threshold-specific metrics elevated to co-primary status.
required_empirical_test.supports_critique_if: The model favored by PR-AUC has precision at top 0.5% OR recall at the budget 3+ percentage points worse than the alternative metric
required_empirical_test.supports_defense_if: Both precision and recall at threshold are within 3 percentage points between models (including PR-AUC-favored model being better or equal)

If both differences are within ±3 percentage points, PR-AUC alone is sufficient; if either difference exceeds 3 percentage points in the PR-AUC-favored model's disfavor, PR-AUC must be demoted and threshold-specific metrics elevated to co-primary status.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: The model favored by PR-AUC has precision at top 0.5% OR recall at the budget 3+ percentage points worse than the alternative metric
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: Both precision and recall at threshold are within 3 percentage points between models (including PR-AUC-favored model being better or equal)

must_not_claim_details.claim: Evaluating only on data from a single organization limits the generalizability of findings
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: Enterprise security model deployment typically occurs in tenant-isolated environments, where data heterogeneity makes cross-tenant generalization less relevant than within-tenant operational performance. The design explicitly targets deployment constraints for a specific production environment.

sound_design_reference:
You would evaluate the hypothesis as a future-incident binary classification problem at the user-day level. The data should cover 18 months of routine production telemetry: identity provider authentication and SSO logs, endpoint/device events, email security events, and analyst-confirmed account-takeover outcomes. Because ground truth is delayed, labels should be generated only where case closure maturity is sufficient. A positive label is assigned to user-days within the pre-investigation detection window for a confirmed account takeover, anchored to the earliest verified compromise onset when available and otherwise to a conservative backdated proxy from investigation open time. User-days with no linked takeover investigation in the following 30 days serve as negatives. Periods with unresolved investigations are excluded.

The split should be strictly chronological. Train on months 1-12, validate on months 13-15, and test on months 16-18, with a 30-day maturity buffer at each boundary. All features must be computed using only information available up to the scoring timestamp, which avoids leakage from future campaign artifacts, retrospective analyst knowledge, or post-incident enrichment.

To make the comparison fair, both models should use the same raw event sources and the same 30-day cutoff window. The tabular pipeline will build per-user/day aggregates such as failed login counts, MFA challenge patterns, impossible-travel flags, IP risk summaries, geovelocity, new-device indicators, endpoint alert rates, mailbox rule creation, unusual sender/recipient behavior, and deviations from each user’s historical baseline. The graph pipeline will use those same raw signals to form a heterogeneous graph over users, devices, IPs, mailboxes, and message entities, with recency-weighted edges and node attributes derived from the same aggregates. Analyst workflow fields, ticket metadata, and any post-investigation annotations are excluded from both pipelines.

The proposed model is a heterogeneous graph neural network with two message-passing layers and class-weighted loss. The baseline is a gradient-boosted tree trained on the matched tabular feature set. Both receive comparable tuning effort on the validation period so the comparison tests representational advantage rather than optimization budget.

Model selection is done on the validation period using PR-AUC as the primary metric. That metric matches the operational setting because account takeover is rare and the SOC consumes a ranked queue under variable daily capacity; the key question is whether true compromises are concentrated near the top of that queue. Secondary reporting should include precision at the top 0.5% of scored user-days, recall at the historical alert budget, AUROC, and calibration via Brier score. The test set is touched once at the end for final reporting only.

To address confounds, hold raw inputs, label logic, and lookback window constant across models. Report subgroup results by campaign cluster, policy segment, and unseen-device prevalence, and run ablations that remove email-derived features from both systems. This design establishes whether the graph formulation improves forward-looking detection performance under realistic deployment constraints; it does not by itself prove which relational mechanism is responsible for any observed gain.
difficulty_justification: The ambiguous choice (primary metric) is identifiable through domain-specific reasoning about operational constraints: a reviewer must recognize that SOC capacity operates at a fixed threshold rather than across the entire ranking curve, and that PR-AUC's theoretical advantages may not align with this practical need. While the choice is not immediately obvious, it is discoverable by tracing the connection between success metrics and real-world deployment constraints described in the design narrative.

hypothesis: For enterprise cybersecurity email defense, a transformer model that combines inbound message text, header features, URL features, and sender-reputation signals will detect phishing emails more accurately than a feature-limited baseline using only metadata and handcrafted rules, especially on novel campaign variants seen in a later time period.
domain: Cybersecurity — enterprise email security using inbound message text, headers, URLs, and sender reputation logs
task_prompt:
You would evaluate the hypothesis on a 12-month stream of real inbound email telemetry captured at delivery time. The training corpus should include subject and body text, parsed headers, authentication results, URL and attachment indicators, and sender/domain reputation signals that were actually available when the message arrived. Labels should come from confirmed phishing verdicts established through analyst review, incident response, or validated user reports. Because some cases are resolved late, use a 30-day label maturation window and drop unresolved messages rather than forcing noisy negatives. This keeps supervision cleaner while still reflecting realistic class imbalance.

The split should be strictly chronological: months 1-8 for training, 9-10 for validation, and 11-12 for test. That is the right design for this domain because phishing tactics, domains, and message templates drift over time, and a later-period test is the only credible way to assess detection of novel campaigns. To further limit contamination, remove exact duplicates and near-duplicate campaign variants that would otherwise appear across windows through replayed templates or shared URL kits.

Feature construction should mirror what the gateway can know before delivery. For text, encode subject plus normalized body content. For structured inputs, include sender/domain age, authentication pass/fail, header inconsistencies, display-name deception indicators, URL lexical and hosting features, attachment type features, and historical sender aggregates computed only from events prior to the message timestamp. Fit all preprocessing objects—tokenizer, vocabularies, imputers, scalers, and any frequency encoders—on training data only, then freeze them for validation and test. Explicitly exclude analyst verdict fields, case metadata, quarantine actions, and any post-delivery user interaction signals because they can encode the label.

Train a multimodal transformer that produces a text representation from subject/body and fuses it with structured features through a classification head. The fair baseline is not a weak rules engine alone; it should be a competitive metadata-only system, such as gradient-boosted trees over headers, authentication, URL, attachment, and handcrafted rule outputs, tuned with the same validation window and similar search effort. That comparison isolates whether adding content and multimodal modeling improves detection beyond mature metadata-driven defenses.

Use partial AUROC in the region up to 0.5% false-positive rate as the primary metric. In this environment, analysts and gateway policies act on the highest-risk slice of mail, so model quality is determined by how well it orders malicious messages above legitimate mail where only a very small false-positive budget is tolerable. Report recall at 0.1% and 0.5% FPR, PR-AUC, full ROC-AUC, and calibration diagnostics as secondary views.

Select hyperparameters and early stopping based on the validation window only; touch the test set once at the end. In addition to overall performance, report results on held-out campaign clusters, business units, and language strata. This experiment would establish whether multimodal transformers improve phishing detection under realistic temporal drift and low-FPR operating conditions. It would not by itself establish robustness to future adversarial adaptation beyond the observed test period or guarantee transportability to organizations with materially different mail distributions.

required_empirical_test.supports_defense_if: Transformer precision is ≥ baseline precision + 5 percentage points at the operational threshold on the test set, validating the partial AUROC-driven prioritization
required_empirical_test.ambiguous_if: Transformer precision is > baseline but ≤ +5 percentage points, indicating marginal gain insufficient to conclusively resolve the dispute

hypothesis: For enterprise cybersecurity vulnerability management, a ranking model that combines asset inventory, network topology, and vulnerability scanner history will surface the vulnerabilities most likely to be remediated or exploited within 30 days better than a baseline ranking model using scanner severity scores alone.
domain: Cybersecurity vulnerability management for enterprise endpoint and server fleets using asset inventory, network topology, and vulnerability scanner data
task_prompt:
You would frame the experiment around newly detected asset-vulnerability findings and evaluate whether richer enterprise context improves prioritization over scanner severity alone. Build the dataset from 18 months of scanner findings joined to CMDB asset metadata, network exposure/topology summaries, patch state, and remediation tickets. The unit of analysis is the first detection of a given asset-vulnerability pair. Repeated scanner observations after first detection are collapsed so the model cannot benefit from later scanner persistence updates. The label is positive if, within 30 days of first detection, the finding is patched, formally suppressed/accepted, or tied to a confirmed exploitation signal; otherwise it is negative. Restrict the cohort to managed endpoints and servers that match the intended deployment scope.

Use a chronological split by first-detection month: 12 months train, 3 months validation, 3 months test. This is the right split for vulnerability management because the scoring problem is explicitly forward-looking: analysts rank fresh findings now and outcomes happen later. Every feature must be timestamp-safe. Compute historical remediation rates, team patch latency, prior CVE-family prevalence, and topology summaries using only data available up to the detection timestamp. Fit imputers, encoders, and any scaling on the training window only, then freeze them for validation and test.

Engineer four feature groups. First, scanner-derived features: severity/CVSS, exploitability flags, finding age, recurrence history, and prior reopen patterns. Second, asset context: asset class, OS family, business criticality, ownership, internet-facing status, and baseline patch compliance. Third, topology/exposure: adjacency counts, segment privilege, reachable vulnerable neighbors, and enclave membership. Fourth, historical remediation behavior available before detection: owner-team median patch latency and asset-level compliance history. Exclude post-detection ticket status, patch timestamps, and any scanner refresh information that occurs after the prediction point.

Train a gradient-boosted learning-to-rank model with pairwise ranking loss on weekly batches of newly detected findings. Compare it against two fair baselines: raw scanner severity ordering and a severity-only regularized logistic model using scanner-native signals. Tune both model families on the same validation window with the same degree of care.

Use mean NDCG@100 across weekly batches as the primary selection and reporting metric. In this workflow, analysts operate from a short weekly queue rather than an exhaustive threshold rule, so the experiment should reward getting the first page of the queue right and penalize burying actionable findings below less urgent ones. Report Precision@25, Precision@100, MAP, and AUROC as secondary diagnostics, with calibration reported if probability outputs are exposed.

Model selection is done strictly on validation. The test window is touched once after architecture, features, and hyperparameters are fixed. To address confounding, report slices by business unit, asset class, and severity band, and compare against baselines that already capture scanner severity and basic criticality. This design can establish whether enterprise context improves prioritization of actionable vulnerabilities in the organization’s actual workflow; it does not, by itself, prove that the model isolates causal exploit risk from organizational remediation policy.

hypothesis: For enterprise cybersecurity phishing detection, a transformer-based model fine-tuned on email body text plus header and URL features will achieve higher event-level F1 than a gradient-boosted tree baseline built on engineered sender, recipient, and lexical statistics, particularly on previously unseen phishing campaigns.
domain: Enterprise cybersecurity email security using inbound message text, headers, URLs, and sender reputation logs
task_prompt:
Use a year of inbound enterprise email data that reflects the actual deployment stream: raw subject and body text, SMTP headers, extracted URLs, attachment metadata, and sender reputation logs, linked to analyst review outcomes, sandbox verdicts, user reports, and downstream incident confirmations. Because phishing labels mature slowly and negatives are partly censored, impose a 21-day maturation window before assigning negatives and exclude the most recent unlabeled mail from all splits. Positives are emails confirmed as phishing through adjudication, malicious sandbox outcome, or later incident linkage within 14 days. Remove exact re-ingests and collapse near-duplicate messages into campaign clusters using normalized body, URL, and sender-template similarity so recurring variants can be tracked.

Split by arrival time, not randomly: train on months 1-8, validate on months 9-10, and test on months 11-12. In addition, assign each near-duplicate campaign cluster to the first split in which it appears so later copies do not leak across train, validation, and test. This directly measures the intended deployment scenario: detecting future phishing traffic, especially campaigns not previously seen. All preprocessing statistics for engineered features are fit on training only; validation and test use frozen transforms. Exclude any fields that arise after message arrival, including analyst notes, user actions after delivery, remediation events, and incident workflow metadata.

Train two models. The proposed model is a multimodal classifier that fine-tunes a pretrained transformer on subject plus body text and concatenates the resulting text embedding with structured header, URL, attachment, and sender-reputation features before a small MLP classification head. The baseline is a strong gradient-boosted tree over the same structured features plus engineered lexical statistics from text, such as token counts, urgency phrases, brand impersonation patterns, obfuscation markers, and URL rarity signals. Tune both models with comparable effort on the validation split only.

The primary evaluation metric is event-level F1. This gives a direct message-level comparison between the multimodal transformer and the boosted-tree detector under severe imbalance, and it rewards models that improve the overall precision-recall balance rather than optimizing only one side of the tradeoff. Thresholds are selected on the validation set and then frozen. In addition to F1, report recall at false-positive rates of 0.1% and 0.01%, precision-recall AUC, and thresholded precision/recall, since security teams care about catching more malicious mail without creating review overload.

Model selection uses validation F1, with early stopping for the transformer and standard hyperparameter search for both systems. The test set is touched once at the end. To address confounds, report performance separately for seen versus unseen campaigns, rerun evaluation with URL/domain reputation features removed to check whether any gain is driven mainly by memorized artifacts, and slice results by label source because analyst-confirmed and user-reported positives have different noise profiles. This experiment establishes whether the transformer-based multimodal detector improves held-out message classification and future-campaign generalization under realistic operational data constraints; it does not by itself prove that any gain comes specifically from deeper semantic understanding rather than better use of residual template signals.

If the higher-F1 model's recall is more than 5 percentage points lower at either FPR threshold, the F1 metric is insufficient as the primary endpoint.

Model selection uses validation F1, with early stopping for the transformer and standard hyperparameter search for both systems. The test set is touched once at the end. hypothesis: For enterprise cybersecurity alert triage, a learning-to-rank model trained on SIEM/EDR alerts plus incident-response feedback will prioritize true incident-causing alerts more effectively than a binary alert classifier with a fixed threshold, improving top-k precision and analyst time-to-resolution.
domain: Cybersecurity operations center (SOC) alert triage using SIEM/EDR alert streams and incident response tickets
task_prompt: Use a retrospective point-in-time study over 18 months of enterprise SOC alert data joined from SIEM, EDR, case-management, and incident-response systems. Build the supervised set at the alert level, but preserve the operational structure of analyst review queues. Labels come from downstream investigation outcomes: alerts linked to confirmed incidents are treated as highest relevance, alerts investigated as suspicious but not escalated receive intermediate relevance, and benign/false-positive closures receive zero relevance. Because many alerts are never reviewed, restrict primary training and evaluation to alerts with explicit analyst or incident outcomes; keep unreviewed alerts out of the supervised benchmark to avoid silently treating unknowns as negatives. Split the data chronologically to respect real drift in attacker behavior, infrastructure, and detection content. Use months 1–12 for training, 13–15 for validation, and 16–18 for test. Keep all alerts tied to the same case or incident in one partition, and compute every aggregate feature in a strict point-in-time fashion so no future analyst action or future incident linkage can leak backward. Engineer features from information available at alert creation: detector metadata, vendor severity, source product, MITRE tags, host and user context, asset criticality, recent alert burst statistics by entity and rule, prior incident involvement up to that timestamp, and textual representations of alert title/description. Exclude any post-triage annotations, ticket routing states, and fields only populated after investigation begins. Train a LambdaMART learning-to-rank model, grouping examples by analyst queue-day so the learner optimizes ordering within realistic daily review batches. This directly matches the operating problem: analysts consume a ranked queue, not isolated probability estimates. For a fair test of the hypothesis, compare it against a gradient-boosted binary classifier trained on the identical feature set, with the same temporal partitions and equivalent tuning effort. The classifier’s scores are converted into a ranking at evaluation time, making the comparison about objective formulation rather than model family or feature advantage. Use NDCG@20 as the primary metric, averaged across queue-days. In this setting the SOC does not need a single hard cutoff as much as it needs the most consequential alerts concentrated near the top of a limited review list, and the graded label structure reflects that a confirmed incident is more valuable than a merely suspicious alert. Report Precision@5, Precision@20, MAP, and AUCPR as secondary views, and estimate operational impact with a replay analysis that measures median time from alert generation to first confirmed incident handling under a fixed analyst review budget. Perform model selection on the validation period only, using NDCG@20. Use grouped, chronology-preserving cross-validation inside the training window to narrow hyperparameters, then retrain on train plus validation only after the configuration is fixed. Touch the test set once at the end and report bootstrap confidence intervals over queue-days. Also provide stratified results by detector family, alert source, and asset criticality, plus a held-out slice for rules first introduced in the test period to check that any ranking gain survives realistic drift.

required_empirical_test.condition: Identify the operational alert-action cutoff in the SOC workflow on the held-out test set by analyzing replayed analyst review logs: determine whether analysts prioritize the top-K alerts per queue-day (median K ≤ 20) or escalate alerts exceeding a fixed score threshold. For the identified cutoff (K or threshold), compute precision of confirmed incident alerts at that operational point for both models (ranking vs. classifier).
required_empirical_test.supports_critique_if: If the absolute precision gap between models at the cutoff exceeds 0.05 (e.g., classifier >0.05 higher precision while NDCG@20 remains comparable), NDCG@20 alone fails to capture critical operational differences
required_empirical_test.supports_defense_if: If the absolute precision gap at the cutoff is ≤ 0.05, NDCG@20 sufficiently captures prioritization effectiveness regardless of operational workflow
required_empirical_test.ambiguous_if: If the median queue-day alert volume is below 10 or multiple distinct cutoff regimes exist (e.g., both top-K and score-based thresholds apply), making strict precision evaluation non-definitive

must_not_claim_details.claim: Excluding unreviewed alerts creates verification bias by ignoring potentially critical alerts.
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: The hypothesis concerns prioritization of alerts that analysts actually review, not discovery of new incident-causing alerts. Labels for unreviewed alerts are missing at random due to analyst workload constraints, not systematically informative. Including unknowns as negatives would penalize models for surfacing novel threats never investigated, violating causal assumptions.

must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: This is a valid concern in general, but in cybersecurity SOC workflows, alerts linked to the same incident share root causes and analyst responses. Preserving incident boundaries ensures realistic leakage prevention, prevents future information bleed during feature computation, and maintains temporal drift simulation critical to deployment realism.

hypothesis: For enterprise software supply-chain security, a graph model that combines package dependency edges, maintainer history, and vulnerability scanner signals will detect vulnerable open-source dependencies better than a tabular baseline built only from package metadata and scanner counts, as measured by PR-AUC on held-out future advisories.
domain: Software supply-chain security analytics for enterprise code repositories and dependency management systems
task_prompt:
Build the experiment around dependency snapshots taken weekly over a 24-month period from enterprise repositories. For each snapshot, create an example for every resolved dependency instance appearing in active codebases, joining manifest and lockfile data with repository metadata, package-manager dependency relations, scanner outputs, public CVE records, and internal advisory logs. Define the prediction task as early warning: given information available on the snapshot date, predict whether that dependency will receive a new vulnerability advisory within the next 90 days. Because disclosure lag can make recent negatives unreliable, drop the final 90 days of raw observations before splitting.

Use a strict chronological split: the earliest 70% of weeks for training, the next 15% for validation, and the final 15% for test. This is the correct deployment-faithful design because both package ecosystems and disclosure patterns drift over time, and the model is intended to score future dependencies rather than interpolate among contemporaneous records. To avoid leakage, compute every feature from information available no later than the snapshot date, fit imputers, encoders, scalers, and graph normalization statistics only on training data, and freeze them afterward. Repeated appearances of the same dependency in adjacent snapshots can remain as separate examples because they represent separate prediction opportunities, but duplicates created by redundant ingestion within a week should be collapsed.

Train a heterogeneous graph neural network over package-version, maintainer, and repository nodes, with edges for dependency relations and maintainer-package links. Use node and edge attributes capturing package age, ecosystem, release cadence, scanner findings by severity, transitive depth, historical maintainer advisory rate, repository security posture, update recency, and popularity buckets. Compute graph-derived statistics such as centrality from the training graph only. Exclude any signal that directly references future advisories, future patch commits, or remediation actions. As the baseline, train a gradient-boosted tree on an aligned tabular feature set containing package metadata, repository metadata, and scanner aggregates but no graph neighborhood information. Give both models comparable tuning effort using the validation window.

Evaluate the final comparison on the held-out future test period using PR-AUC as the primary metric. The practical objective is to rank truly risky dependencies near the top so analysts can review the most concerning items first under tight capacity constraints; in a heavily imbalanced security setting, this ranking quality is better reflected by PR-AUC than by metrics dominated by abundant negatives. Report recall at the top 1%, 5%, and 10% of scored dependencies, precision at a top-K corresponding to weekly analyst budget, ROC-AUC, and calibration diagnostics as secondary views.

Select hyperparameters solely on validation PR-AUC, lock the full pipeline, and touch the test set once. To address confounds, report performance by package popularity, repository size, ecosystem, and whether a dependency is newly introduced or long-lived. Also run ablations removing popularity and maintainer-history features and compare results within popularity-matched strata. This experiment establishes whether relational structure improves future vulnerability ranking beyond strong tabular security signals; it does not by itself prove causal mechanisms behind why a package becomes vulnerable.

ground_truth.final_verdict: Empirically contested — the design choice at issue cannot be validated without measurement

required_empirical_test.condition: Compute the proportion of operational scoring runs using a fixed threshold or weekly review budget within 10% of the mean value in historical triage logs. If at least 80% of scoring runs fall into this category, measure the graph model's precision at that threshold/top-K and compare to the baseline's precision. A 5 percentage point deficit or greater at the operational point indicates PR-AUC is insufficient as the sole primary metric.
required_empirical_test.supports_critique_if: At least 80% of operational cases use a fixed threshold or stable review budget AND the graph model's precision at that point is lower than the baseline by ≥5 percentage points
required_empirical_test.supports_defense_if: Fewer than 80% of cases use fixed/stable thresholds OR the graph model's precision deficit is ≤5 percentage points at the stable operating point

ideal_debate_resolution.condition: Compute the proportion of operational scoring runs using a fixed threshold or weekly review budget within 10% of the mean value in historical triage logs. A 5 percentage point deficit or greater at the operational point indicates PR-AUC is insufficient as the sole primary metric.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: Fewer than 80% of cases use fixed/stable thresholds OR the graph model's precision deficit is ≤5 percentage points at the stable operating point

must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: The 90-day horizon followed by dropping the latest 90 days of raw data ensures all negative examples have completed their observation window, maintaining label integrity. Shorter horizons risk incomplete disclosure information in operational deployment.

must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: Chronological split matches deployment conditions where future package ecosystems and vulnerability patterns must be generalized, and prevents leakage from time-dependent features and training statistics.
