hypothesis: For enterprise cybersecurity threat detection, a graph-based model that incorporates identity, device, and email interaction links will detect account-takeover incidents more accurately than a feature-only gradient-boosted baseline when evaluated on future incident windows.
domain: Cybersecurity threat detection for enterprise account takeover investigation using identity provider logs, device telemetry, and email security events
task_prompt:
You would evaluate the hypothesis with a time-respecting comparison between a heterogeneous graph model and a strong feature-only gradient-boosted baseline on enterprise account-takeover detection. Start from 12 months of identity provider logs, endpoint telemetry, email security events, and analyst-confirmed investigation outcomes. Define each prediction unit as a user/session or 24-hour alert window, labeled positive when the investigation is closed as account takeover and negative when reviewed and closed as benign or non-ATO. Exclude unresolved cases and any examples whose labels are not available by the supervision cutoff, since delayed security investigations otherwise inject label noise.

Use a chronological split to match deployment on future attack windows: months 1–8 for training, 9–10 for validation, and 11–12 for test. This is necessary because attack tooling, infrastructure, and user behavior drift over time. To limit campaign leakage, keep exact case or campaign identifiers inside one split and remove features tied to analyst workflow timing or case closure state. All imputers, category thresholds, normalization parameters, and any learned preprocessing are fit on training only and then frozen.

For the baseline, build a tabular feature set from signals available at scoring time: login failure and MFA patterns, impossible-travel indicators, first-seen device/IP flags, device health and posture, sender novelty, burstiness of inbound email, mailbox rule changes, and historical user risk aggregates. For the graph model, use the same node attributes but add typed edges capturing user-device authentications, user-IP associations, user-sender contacts, and device-email relationships, with recency and interaction-count weights. Construct graphs using only data available up to the relevant split boundary so future edges never enter training or inference.

Train a heterogeneous graph neural network with message passing across user, device, IP, and sender nodes and a binary classification head. Compare it against a gradient-boosted tree baseline tuned with the same discipline: validation-based search over learning rate, depth, number of trees, sampling parameters, and class weighting. This keeps the experiment focused on whether relational structure adds predictive value beyond strong hand-engineered aggregates.

Select models on the validation window using average precision as the primary metric. In this setting the analyst team triages a ranked queue under heavy class imbalance, so concentrating true takeovers near the top of the score distribution is the most useful behavior. Also report recall at fixed precision levels, precision in the top 100 and top 500 alerts per week, AUROC, and calibration diagnostics. The final 2-month test window is touched once after model family and hyperparameters are locked, and performance differences are summarized with bootstrap confidence intervals.

To address confounds, run ablations removing raw high-cardinality identifiers while preserving behavioral summaries, and report results separately for previously seen versus newly observed users and devices. This experiment will establish whether relational modeling improves future-window detection under realistic drift and label delay; it will not by itself prove that every observed gain comes from abstract graph reasoning rather than specific recurrent entity patterns.

ground_truth.final_verdict: Empirically contested — the design choice at issue cannot be validated without measurement

required_empirical_test.condition: Compare the graph model and baseline model's recall at the operational precision threshold (e.g., 0.6 or 0.8) and precision at the operational top-K budget (e.g., top 100 alerts per week) on the test set. If the model with higher average precision underperforms the alternative by more than 5 percentage points in either recall at precision floor or precision at top-K, average precision is insufficient as the primary metric.
required_empirical_test.supports_critique_if: The graph or baseline model with higher average precision is worse by >5 percentage points in recall at the operational precision floor or precision at the operational top-K.
required_empirical_test.supports_defense_if: The model with higher average precision is not worse by >5 percentage points in either metric.

ideal_debate_resolution.condition: Compare the graph model and baseline model's recall at the operational precision threshold (e.g., 0.6 or 0.8) and precision at the operational top-K budget (e.g., top 100 alerts per week) on the test set. If the model with higher average precision underperforms the alternative by more than 5 percentage points in either recall at precision floor or precision at top-K, average precision is insufficient as the primary metric.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: The graph or baseline model with higher average precision is worse by >5 percentage points in recall at the operational precision floor or precision at the operational top-K.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: The model with higher average precision is not worse by >5 percentage points in either metric.

must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: This control ensures that performance gains are attributed to relational reasoning rather than access to raw identifiers. Behavioral summaries are sufficient for predictions while preventing confounding.

hypothesis: For enterprise cybersecurity vulnerability management, a learning-to-rank model that combines asset inventory, network topology, and vulnerability scanner severity signals will prioritize truly exploitable hosts more effectively than ranking by CVSS score alone, yielding higher recall@k for confirmed remediation actions on held-out weeks.
domain: Cybersecurity vulnerability management for enterprise endpoint and server fleets using asset inventory, network topology, and vulnerability scanner data
task_prompt:
Build the experiment around weekly ranking episodes, because that is how vulnerability management is actually run. For each week in an 18-month history, create the candidate set of open asset-vulnerability findings visible to the security team at that time. Join scanner outputs, CMDB attributes, network topology summaries, and remediation system events using only information timestamped on or before the scoring week. Define relevance from downstream operational evidence: a finding is relevant if it is confirmed remediated within 21 days of entering the queue or if it is escalated through incident-response validation in that window. This is not a perfect exploitability label, but it is the best decision-proximal outcome available and is aligned with what the team ultimately acts on.

Use a strict chronological split: train on the earliest 12 months, tune on the next 3 months, and reserve the final 3 months for test. This avoids leakage from future patching behavior, future asset classifications, and evolving network state. Any preprocessing that learns from data distribution, including imputation, scaling, or frequency encoding, is fit on the training window only. Graph features such as exposure counts, vulnerable-neighbor counts, and centrality summaries are recomputed per week from the network data available at that point in time.

Train a gradient-boosted learning-to-rank model with weekly query groups so the model directly learns within-week prioritization. The main comparison should be against the operational baseline of sorting by CVSS score, with deterministic vendor-severity tie-breaks. Also include a stronger non-ranking baseline: logistic regression on the same tabular non-graph signals with comparable hyperparameter tuning effort. That establishes whether any gain comes from the richer ranking formulation and nonlinear interactions rather than from simply adding obvious metadata.

Evaluate primarily with NDCG@100. In this setting, the team reviews only a bounded top slice of the weekly queue, and the practical objective is to concentrate the findings most likely to warrant action near the very top, not merely to improve average ordering over the full tail. NDCG@100 captures both truncation and position sensitivity, which is appropriate for analyst workflows with finite triage bandwidth. Report Recall@100, Recall@50, and MAP as secondary metrics so the result can also be interpreted in terms of coverage of acted-on findings.

Select hyperparameters using mean validation NDCG@100 across weeks, then retrain on train plus validation with the chosen settings and score the untouched test weeks once. To address confounding, run ablations for severity-only, asset-only, and asset-plus-exposure inputs, and report stratified results for internet-exposed versus internal assets and for high- versus lower-criticality systems. This experiment will establish whether combining inventory, topology, and scanner signals improves operational prioritization over CVSS alone on future weeks. It will not establish causal exploitability in the absence of action-label bias, so conclusions should remain about prioritization effectiveness under real enterprise workflow constraints.

required_empirical_test.condition: Compute the coefficient of variation (CV) of historical weekly review depths (K). If ≥80% of weekly remediation actions fall within a fixed K with CV(K) ≤ 0.2, measure the absolute difference between the model's Recall@K and NDCG@100 rankings on validation data. If this difference exceeds 5 percentage points, NDCG@100 alone is an insufficient primary metric.
required_empirical_test.supports_critique_if: Fixed K stability (CV(K) ≤ 0.2 with ≥80% coverage) and absolute ranking discrepancy >5 percentage points
required_empirical_test.supports_defense_if: Either (a) fixed K stability not met or (b) ranking discrepancy ≤5 percentage points under stable K
required_empirical_test.ambiguous_if: Historical K coverage between 75–85% with CV between 0.15–0.25 and ranking discrepancy between 4–6 percentage points

ideal_debate_resolution.condition: Compute the coefficient of variation (CV) of historical weekly review depths (K). If ≥80% of weekly remediation actions fall within a fixed K with CV(K) ≤ 0.2, measure the absolute difference between the model's Recall@K and NDCG@100 rankings on validation data. If this difference exceeds 5 percentage points, NDCG@100 alone is an insufficient primary metric.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: Fixed K stability (CV(K) ≤ 0.2 with ≥80% coverage) and absolute ranking discrepancy >5 percentage points
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: Either (a) fixed K stability not met or (b) ranking discrepancy ≤5 percentage points under stable K
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: Historical K coverage between 75–85% with CV between 0.15–0.25 and ranking discrepancy between 4–6 percentage points

must_not_claim_details.claim: Chronological splitting artificially constrains generalization to future asset types.
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: Chronological validation prevents data leakage in time-dependent decision flows, which reflects real deployment conditions where models score new weekly findings using only prior data.

must_not_claim_details.claim: Neighborhood exposure counts and graph features risk overfitting to rare network configurations.
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: Exposure from network neighbors directly influences exploit potential in enterprise environments; these features enable the model to capture cascading risk and asset relationships that drive real-world attack surface considerations.

resolution_descriptions.empirical_test_agreed: Passing verdict — debate recognized the ambiguity and specified an empirical test to resolve it

sound_design_reference:
Build the experiment around weekly ranking episodes, because that is how vulnerability management is actually run. For each week in an 18-month history, create the candidate set of open asset-vulnerability findings visible to the security team at that time. Join scanner outputs, CMDB attributes, network topology summaries, and remediation system events using only information timestamped on or before the scoring week. Define relevance from downstream operational evidence: a finding is relevant if it is confirmed remediated within 21 days of entering the queue or if it is escalated through incident-response validation in that window. This is not a perfect exploitability label, but it is the best decision-proximal outcome available and is aligned with what the team ultimately acts on.

Use a strict chronological split: train on the earliest 12 months, tune on the next 3 months, and reserve the final 3 months for test. This avoids leakage from future patching behavior, future asset classifications, and evolving network state. Any preprocessing that learns from data distribution, including imputation, scaling, or frequency encoding, is fit on the training window only. Graph features such as exposure counts, vulnerable-neighbor counts, and centrality summaries are recomputed per week from the network data available at that point in time.

Train a gradient-boosted learning-to-rank model with weekly query groups so the model directly learns within-week prioritization. The main comparison should be against the operational baseline of sorting by CVSS score, with deterministic vendor-severity tie-breaks. Also include a stronger non-ranking baseline: logistic regression on the same tabular non-graph signals with comparable hyperparameter tuning effort. That establishes whether any gain comes from the richer ranking formulation and nonlinear interactions rather than from simply adding obvious metadata.

Evaluate primarily with NDCG@100. In this setting, the team reviews only a bounded top slice of the weekly queue, and the practical objective is to concentrate the findings most likely to warrant action near the very top, not merely to improve average ordering over the full tail. NDCG@100 captures both truncation and position sensitivity, which is appropriate for analyst workflows with finite triage bandwidth. Report Recall@100, Recall@50, and MAP as secondary metrics so the result can also be interpreted in terms of coverage of acted-on findings.

Select hyperparameters using mean validation NDCG@100 across weeks, then retrain on train plus validation with the chosen settings and score the untouched test weeks once. To address confounding, run ablations for severity-only, asset-only, and asset-plus-exposure inputs, and report stratified results for internet-exposed versus internal assets and for high- versus lower-criticality systems. This experiment will establish whether combining inventory, topology, and scanner signals improves operational prioritization over CVSS alone on future weeks. It will not establish causal exploitability in the absence of action-label bias, so conclusions should remain about prioritization effectiveness under real enterprise workflow constraints.
difficulty_justification: The ambiguous choice centers on primary metric selection. A methodologically aware reviewer can trace the hypothesis's emphasis on recall@k versus the design's use of NDCG@100 and operational justification. Validating this requires understanding how ranking metrics align with team workflows and cutoffs, but does not demand deep cybersecurity expertise.

hypothesis: For a large hospital network, a transformer model that jointly encodes free-text chief complaints, triage notes, and the first 6 hours of vitals/labs will predict 24-hour emergency department admission better than a tabular gradient-boosted baseline trained on structured triage variables alone.
domain: Healthcare / emergency department operations using triage records, free-text chief complaints, and early bedside/lab data from a multi-hospital health system
task_prompt:
Use a retrospective cohort of ED encounters from all hospitals in the system over four years. Define the prediction time uniformly at 6 hours after ED arrival and include only data that would have been available by that point: demographics, arrival mode, triage acuity, chief complaint, triage note, timestamped vitals, and ordered/resulted labs up to 6 hours. The outcome is inpatient admission within 24 hours of arrival, taken from administrative disposition and admission-order records. Exclude encounters with invalid timestamps, missing encounter linkage, or dispositions that are operationally non-comparable, such as direct transfers already assigned to inpatient care before ED evaluation. Standardize lab units and deduplicate near-simultaneous measurements using pre-specified clinical rules.

Split the data at the encounter level into 70/15/15 train, validation, and test partitions, stratified by hospital and admission status. Assign all encounters from the same patient to a single partition to prevent repeat-patient leakage. This split preserves each site’s case mix and admission prevalence in every partition, which is important because the objective is to compare how much additional early clinical signal the multimodal model extracts, not to measure adaptation to a changing policy regime. Leakage is prevented by censoring every feature strictly at the 6-hour mark and excluding bed requests, consultant disposition recommendations, post-6-hour orders, and any text entered after the prediction time.

Construct two model families. The proposed model is a multimodal transformer that jointly encodes chief complaint text, triage notes, and the ordered sequence of vitals/labs/events from the first 6 hours, fused with static structured fields. Relative-time embeddings capture when measurements occurred, and missingness indicators are retained because test-ordering behavior is itself informative. The main baseline is gradient-boosted trees on structured variables available by 6 hours, including triage data, initial vitals, abnormality flags, and early lab-order indicators, but excluding free text. Tune both models with comparable effort on the validation set.

Use AUROC as the primary comparison metric because the operational need is to sort the active ED census by admission likelihood so bed managers can prioritize attention under shifting capacity constraints; the exact intervention threshold will vary by hospital and by hour. Report AUPRC because prevalence may differ substantially across sites, and report Brier score, calibration slope/intercept, expected calibration error, and threshold-based PPV/recall at pre-specified high-risk cutoffs to characterize whether the ranking remains clinically usable at the top end.

Select hyperparameters and perform early stopping on validation only. After locking the configuration, refit on train plus validation and evaluate once on the held-out test set. Report pooled results and hospital-stratified results, plus subgroup checks by acuity, shift, interpreter use, and note length. This design will establish whether jointly modeling text and early physiological trajectory improves early admission discrimination beyond structured triage variables alone; it will not establish causal benefit in operations without a downstream deployment study.

ground_truth.final_verdict: Empirically contested — the design choice at issue cannot be validated without measurement

required_empirical_test.condition: Compute precision@hospital-specific K at the capped top fraction of the ED census reviewed for escalation decisions, comparing the transformer and baseline models on the held-out test set. If precision@K for the transformer is more than 0.03 absolute lower than the baseline at the operational K threshold, AUROC alone is insufficient as the primary metric.
required_empirical_test.supports_critique_if: Transformer's precision@K falls >0.03 absolute below the baseline at the deployed K threshold
required_empirical_test.supports_defense_if: Transformer's precision@K is within 0.03 absolute of or exceeds the baseline at the deployed K threshold

ideal_debate_resolution.condition: Compute precision@hospital-specific K at the capped top fraction of the ED census reviewed for escalation decisions, comparing the transformer and baseline models on the held-out test set. If precision@K for the transformer is more than 0.03 absolute lower than the baseline at the operational K threshold, AUROC alone is insufficient as the primary metric.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: Transformer's precision@K falls >0.03 absolute below the baseline at the deployed K threshold
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: Transformer's precision@K is within 0.03 absolute of or exceeds the baseline at the deployed K threshold

must_not_claim_details.claim: Using stratified random splits instead of chronological splits compromises temporal validity
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: The hypothesis specifically tests cross-sectional clinical signal present at the 6-hour prediction horizon, not secular trends over time, making stratified splits appropriate to preserve site mix representation

must_not_claim_details.claim: Retaining missingness indicators introduces noise into the model inputs
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: Ordering patterns of labs/vitals contain clinical workflow information — missingness itself is operationally meaningful as it reflects test prioritization

hypothesis: In enterprise cybersecurity SOC alert triage, a learning-to-rank model that incorporates alert graph context, asset criticality, and recent incident-response labels will prioritize true incident alerts better than a model that ranks using alert content and vendor severity scores alone, as measured by precision at the top-K alerts per analyst shift.
domain: Cybersecurity operations center (SOC) alert triage for enterprise network intrusion detection
task_prompt:
Use 18 months of historical SOC data from the enterprise SIEM/EDR environment, joined across alert telemetry, analyst disposition logs, incident-response tickets, asset inventory, and containment records. The target should reflect operationally meaningful relevance rather than raw analyst closure status, so define a positive item as an alert that is escalated to or linked with a confirmed incident within 72 hours of alert creation. This avoids treating workflow-specific benign closures as the truth signal. Because analyst labels can lag alert time, assign labels only from downstream events inside that post-alert window and drop any examples whose labeling window would cross a partition boundary.

Split the data strictly by time: 12 months train, 3 months validation, 3 months test. This is the correct deployment-faithful choice because the model will rank newly arriving alerts using only information available at fire time, and the alert stream, analyst behavior, and attacker activity all drift over time. Group ranking examples by analyst shift so each query corresponds to the set of alerts competing for attention in an actual review period. Leakage controls are straightforward: all graph features, recency counts, and incident-history aggregates are computed with left-closed windows ending at the alert timestamp; preprocessing statistics are fit on training only and reused unchanged downstream.

Train a LambdaMART ranker. The baseline should not be weak: use the same ranking model class, same query grouping, and the same hyperparameter budget, but restrict inputs to alert content and vendor severity family features. This makes the comparison answer the intended question—whether graph context, asset criticality, and recent IR labels add value beyond the information analysts already get from the alert itself. Include two ablations as controls: one that adds asset criticality without graph context, and one that adds recent label-history features without graph structure. Those isolate whether any gain is coming from obvious operational priors rather than the relational context claim in the hypothesis.

Construct baseline features from rule signature, source product, vendor severity, MITRE tags, host and user metadata, and cyclical time features, plus compact text or categorical representations of alert content. The proposed model adds graph-derived features over a 30-day pre-alert entity graph: node degree, repeated co-occurrence counts, neighborhood incident rates, prior containment on related entities, and asset criticality/ownership tier. Recent incident-response labels are aggregated only from events observed before the current alert.

Evaluate primarily with Precision@K, where K equals the median number of alerts an analyst reviews in the first 30 minutes of a shift. That aligns the metric to the first-screen prioritization problem SOC teams actually face. Report NDCG@K, Recall@K, and MRR as secondary views of ranking quality. Select hyperparameters on the validation block using mean Precision@K across shifts, then lock the design and score the final comparison on the untouched test period with bootstrap confidence intervals over shifts. Also report results stratified by source product and asset tier to verify that improvements are not concentrated in one detector family or one class of critical assets.

ground_truth.final_verdict: Empirically contested — the design choice at issue cannot be validated without measurement

required_empirical_test.condition: Train the same proposed ranker with graph/context features computed over 30-day and 90-day lookback windows, keeping all other settings fixed, and compare validation Precision@K averaged across analyst shifts. Compute the difference with bootstrap confidence intervals over shifts to account for variation in daily workloads and analyst attention windows.
required_empirical_test.supports_critique_if: The 90-day window improves Precision@K by more than 0.02 absolute over the 30-day window on the validation set
required_empirical_test.supports_defense_if: The 90-day window improves Precision@K by 0.02 absolute or less over the 30-day window on the validation set
required_empirical_test.ambiguous_if: The point estimate shows improvement but the 90% confidence interval includes the 0.02 threshold

ideal_debate_resolution.condition: Train the same proposed ranker with graph/context features computed over 30-day and 90-day lookback windows, keeping all other settings fixed, and compare validation Precision@K averaged across analyst shifts. Compute the difference with bootstrap confidence intervals over shifts to account for variation in daily workloads and analyst attention windows.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: The 90-day window improves Precision@K by more than 0.02 absolute over the 30-day window on the validation set
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: The 90-day window improves Precision@K by 0.02 absolute or less over the 30-day window on the validation set
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: The point estimate shows improvement but the 90% confidence interval includes the 0.02 threshold

must_not_claim_details.claim: Grouping of training examples by analyst shift degrades model performance as ranking across unrelated alerts from different shifts is more straightforward
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: The shift grouping is necessary because the operational goal is to optimize alert order within the fixed window of an analyst's initial attention period during a shift. Any alternative grouping would misrepresent the actual decision boundary in the enterprise SOC workflow.

must_not_claim_details.claim: Using Precision@K as the primary evaluation metric rather than NDCG causes the model to ignore the relative importance of highly relevant alerts in deeper positions
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: Precision@K directly measures whether the most actionable alerts (true positives) appear in the first screen an analyst sees during the initial 30 minutes of their shift, which is the primary operational constraint in this environment. Any alternative would misalign with the real-world use case.

hypothesis: For a large hospital network’s emergency department triage workflow, a model that combines structured EHR variables with chief complaint text embeddings will predict 72-hour ED revisit risk more accurately than a structured-data-only baseline, yielding a higher AUROC and better calibration on a temporally held-out future month.
domain: Healthcare / emergency department operations using triage records, free-text chief complaints, and prior encounter history from a multi-hospital health system
task_prompt:
Use a retrospective cohort of discharged ED visits from the health system’s last 36 months of data. Restrict the cohort to encounters where the model could realistically be used in triage-to-discharge workflow: visits with a completed triage record, a valid chief complaint field, and a final disposition of discharge. Define the label as a return ED visit anywhere in the same network within 72 hours of discharge from the index encounter. Exclude admissions, transfers, deaths, and visits leaving before evaluation because the intervention target is discharged patients.

Respect the stated deployment setting with a strict chronological split: 28 months train, 4 months validation, 4 months test. This directly addresses temporal drift in revisit behavior, documentation practice, and operational policy. All preprocessing is fit on training data only. Missing structured values are imputed within the pipeline; continuous variables are scaled only where needed; categorical encodings and any text normalization rules are frozen after training. Patient history features are computed using data available strictly before the index visit, and no post-discharge orders, diagnosis finalization fields, or utilization events occurring after the prediction timestamp are included.

Construct the structured feature set from variables available by the end of triage or early visit flow: demographics, arrival mode, triage vitals, triage acuity, insurance, site, visit time context, prior ED and inpatient utilization counts, prior diagnosis group indicators already on record, and medication burden. For text, generate fixed dense embeddings from the raw chief complaint string after lowercasing, punctuation cleanup, and removal of obvious templated boilerplate. Include complaint length as a separate covariate to control for verbosity effects.

Train a gradient-boosted tree model on the combined structured and text features. Use the same model family for the baseline, differing only by removal of text embeddings. That keeps the comparison focused on whether chief complaint text contributes incremental predictive information beyond structured triage and utilization history rather than conflating feature modality with model class. Run matched hyperparameter searches for both models on the validation set. After selecting the best configuration, fit isotonic calibration on validation predictions for each model before final test evaluation.

Use AUROC as the primary metric on the temporally held-out final 4 months. In this workflow, the score is used to rank discharged patients for limited follow-up resources whose daily cutoff moves with staffing and census, so global ordering of risk is the core requirement. Also report AUPRC because revisits are uncommon, along with Brier score, calibration slope/intercept, expected calibration error, and sensitivity/PPV in the top risk decile to show whether improved ranking is accompanied by usable probability estimates.

Control for known confounds by including site indicators, reporting performance by hospital and by month, and checking whether any text-related gain remains within site rather than being driven by coding conventions. The experiment establishes whether chief complaint embeddings add predictive value in this health system under future-month deployment conditions; it does not, by itself, establish causal effects on revisit reduction or generalization to out-of-network returns.

ground_truth.final_verdict: Empirically contested — the design choice at issue cannot be validated without measurement

required_empirical_test.condition: Analyze operational logs from the pilot implementation phase to determine if at least 80% of intervention days use a stable review capacity within +/-2 percentage points of a single cutoff. If this operational stability threshold is met, compute PPV at this fixed threshold (e.g., top 10% decile) on the test set and verify whether the structured-plus-text model improves PPV by at least 0.02 absolute compared to the baseline.
required_empirical_test.supports_critique_if: The pilot logs show a stable operating cutoff meeting the 80% threshold AND the PPV improvement is less than 0.02
required_empirical_test.supports_defense_if: Either (a) the pilot logs show inconsistent operating thresholds (less than 80% stable), OR (b) PPV improves by ≥0.02 at the stable threshold
required_empirical_test.ambiguous_if: The pilot logs show exactly 80% stability with PPV improvement of exactly 0.02

hypothesis: For retail banking anti-money-laundering monitoring, a graph-based model that uses account-network relationships and transaction-merchant metadata will detect suspicious retail and small-business accounts better than a transaction-only gradient-boosted baseline, as measured by precision at a fixed investigator-review budget.
domain: Retail banking anti-money-laundering monitoring for retail and small-business accounts
task_prompt:
Use a retrospective account-month prediction design that mirrors deployment. For each month in a 36-month historical window, create a scoring snapshot for every active retail or small-business account with at least 60 days of prior activity. The label is whether that account reaches a confirmed suspicious AML outcome within the next 90 days, based on analyst-confirmed cases, SAR filings, or AML-related closures. Because labels are delayed, enforce a 90-day maturation buffer so every example has a fully observed outcome window before it can enter train, validation, or test. Exclude accounts with unresolved investigations at censoring.

Split the data strictly by time: 24 months train, 6 months validation, 6 months test. This is the right choice for AML because typologies, investigator behavior, and customer mix drift over time, and any design that mixes future periods into training would overstate real monitoring performance. Leakage control is central: all rolling aggregates are computed from events observed before the monthly scoring cutoff, graph edges are built only from pre-cutoff transactions and relationships, and no case outcomes, SAR text, closure reasons, or post-investigation artifacts are allowed into features.

Train two systems. The baseline is a tuned gradient-boosted tree on transaction and profile features only: transaction velocity, amount bands, cash intensity, merchant-category mix, inbound/outbound asymmetry, counterparty concentration, reversal patterns, account age, geography mismatch, and prior alert counts known at the scoring time. The proposed model is a heterogeneous graph neural network over accounts, merchants, and counterparties, using temporally filtered edges from transfers, card payments, and repeated transactional ties. In addition to the tabular signals, it learns from neighborhood structure, shared-counterparty motifs, merchant-neighborhood risk concentration, and ego-network flow imbalance.

Select hyperparameters on the validation window only, using mean monthly precision at the top-K ranked accounts, where K is the fixed investigator review budget per month. This is the primary metric because the operational decision is not to classify every account at a threshold; it is to fill a limited analyst queue with the most suspicious candidates. A model is useful if it produces more confirmed suspicious accounts within that queue. Report recall at top-K, PR-AUC, and alerts-per-hit as secondary metrics, plus segment-level results for retail and small-business accounts.

To control confounds, keep feature availability matched at the scoring timestamp, and run an ablation that removes merchant metadata from the graph model to isolate the value of network structure itself. Also report performance by month, account tenure, and customer segment to check whether gains are concentrated in one subpopulation or vanish under drift. This experiment will establish whether relational modeling improves investigator-yield under a fixed review budget relative to a strong transaction-only baseline; it will not establish causal deterrence effects or completeness of AML labels.

ground_truth.final_verdict: Empirically contested — the design choice at issue cannot be validated without measurement

required_empirical_test.condition: On the validation data, compute precision at both the production alert threshold and the top-K cutoff for the graph model and baseline. If the absolute difference in graph-baseline precision gaps between (a) threshold-based and (b) top-K-based evaluation exceeds 3 percentage points, then precision at top-K alone is insufficient.
required_empirical_test.supports_critique_if: If precision comparison at the alert threshold yields a graph-baseline gap that is more than 3 percentage points higher or lower than their precision-at-top-K gap

must_not_claim_details.claim: Restricting evaluation to accounts with 60+ days of activity reduces training data volume and risks underrepresenting newer accounts
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: The 60-day threshold ensures sufficient behavioral history to detect AML patterns while avoiding noise from short-lived accounts, which is essential for models learning temporal relationships

must_not_claim_details.claim: Strict chronological splits prevent effective model training by limiting the ability to learn from recent data patterns
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: Time-based splits are mandatory for AML due to temporal drift in typologies, regulator requirements, and deployment realism where models train on past data and evaluate future cases

resolution_descriptions.empirical_test_agreed: Passing verdict — debate recognized the ambiguity and specified an empirical test to resolve it

sound_design_reference:
Use a retrospective account-month prediction design that mirrors deployment. For each month in a 36-month historical window, create a scoring snapshot for every active retail or small-business account with at least 60 days of prior activity. The label is whether that account reaches a confirmed suspicious AML outcome within the next 90 days, based on analyst-confirmed cases, SAR filings, or AML-related closures. Because labels are delayed, enforce a 90-day maturation buffer so every example has a fully observed outcome window before it can enter train, validation, or test. Exclude accounts with unresolved investigations at censoring.

Split the data strictly by time: 24 months train, 6 months validation, 6 months test. This is the right choice for AML because typologies, investigator behavior, and customer mix drift over time, and any design that mixes future periods into training would overstate real monitoring performance. Leakage control is central: all rolling aggregates are computed from events observed before the monthly scoring cutoff, graph edges are built only from pre-cutoff transactions and relationships, and no case outcomes, SAR text, closure reasons, or post-investigation artifacts are allowed into features.

Train two systems. The baseline is a tuned gradient-boosted tree on transaction and profile features only: transaction velocity, amount bands, cash intensity, merchant-category mix, inbound/outbound asymmetry, counterparty concentration, reversal patterns, account age, geography mismatch, and prior alert counts known at the scoring time. The proposed model is a heterogeneous graph neural network over accounts, merchants, and counterparties, using temporally filtered edges from transfers, card payments, and repeated transactional ties. In addition to the tabular signals, it learns from neighborhood structure, shared-counterparty motifs, merchant-neighborhood risk concentration, and ego-network flow imbalance.

Select hyperparameters on the validation window only, using mean monthly precision at the top-K ranked accounts, where K is the fixed investigator review budget per month. This is the primary metric because the operational decision is not to classify every account at a threshold; it is to fill a limited analyst queue with the most suspicious candidates. A model is useful if it produces more confirmed suspicious accounts within that queue. Report recall at top-K, PR-AUC, and alerts-per-hit as secondary metrics, plus segment-level results for retail and small-business accounts.

To control confounds, keep feature availability matched at the scoring timestamp, and run an ablation that removes merchant metadata from the graph model to isolate the value of network structure itself. Also report performance by month, account tenure, and customer segment to check whether gains are concentrated in one subpopulation or vanish under drift. This experiment will establish whether relational modeling improves investigator-yield under a fixed review budget relative to a strong transaction-only baseline; it will not establish causal deterrence effects or completeness of AML labels.
difficulty_justification: The ambiguous choice about metric alignment requires connecting the hypothesis's stated focus on analyst queues (top-K) to deployment realities where thresholds might govern queue composition. Reviewers need to trace the evaluation logic and recognize that threshold sensitivity is a valid concern without assuming specific AML domain knowledge, making this a medium-level ambiguity.
